EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow spread across the region on the 12th. As the snow was ending, northwest winds increased and temperatures dropped below zero on the 13th. There was blowing snow across western and central Minnesota, but the lack of snow on the ground kept travel impacts to a minimum. Overnight wind chills fell to near 40 below across western and central Minnesota the night of the 13th into the 14th.||Based on discussions with other forecasters, emergency managers, and Minnesota Department of Transportation, this would have been a high-impact blizzard if there was a deep snow pack.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 36 mph at the Glenwood airport, with blowing snow and reduced visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered clusters of storms resulted in large hail over portions of east-central Minnesota during the afternoon on July 13. One storm produced a brief tornado in Rice County. A line of storms then developed over northwestern Minnesota during the evening and travelled southeast across central Minnesota throughout the night. This line led to numerous reports of hail and wind damage before it weakened as it moved into Wisconsin.||An unusually warm and humid air mass resulted in heat index values greater than 100F across portions of southern and western Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of July 13. The combination of several thunderstorms and persistent cloud cover kept heat index values in the low to mid 80s further east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Relayed from Metro Skywarn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the Upper Midwest from the 10th into the 11th. The low track set up easterly winds over Lake Superior leading to terrain and lake effect snowfall along the North Shore of Lake Superior. This led to snowfall totals of 7 to 14 inches in this area with a high report of 15 inches in Finland. Additionally, a long duration light snowfall more inland across St. Louis County led to snowfall totals of 6 to 9 inches in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration snowfall event occurred starting the afternoon of the 10th and continued through the afternoon of the 11th. Winds turned easterly off Lake Superior during the late afternoon and brought additional moisture to the area along with terrain effects to keep the snowfall going. Just over 6 inches of snow fell by the next morning with additional accumulation through the day leading to totals of 5 to 7 inches. The highest report was 7.1 inches near Knife River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of Koochiching, Itasca, Cass, Crow Wing and Aitkin counties during January. No change in the area of D2 Severe Drought was observed through the month. Precipitation for the month ended up near to below normal over most of the area. Very little in terms of snow cover was observed through the month and noticeably dry conditions persisted throughout.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted through the month across the southwestern corner of the county. Precipitation for the month was around a half inch below normal over most of the county with a tiny area of near normal precipitation in the far northwestern corner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave propagating along the Canadian border brought strong cold air advection into the area, increasing wind speeds along with falling snow. In addition, snowfall in Canada was easily lofted and blown into our CWA by the high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CO-OP observer reports 100-500 foot visibility from Lancaster to Hallock. Started around sunrise and is still ongoing, with travel nearly impossible. In addition, HCO AWOS got down to 0.5 SM at its lowest and RWIS sites around Kittson county had visibility decrease down to 1/4SM during the morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave propagating along the Canadian border brought strong cold air advection into the area, increasing wind speeds along with falling snow. In addition, snowfall in Canada was easily lofted and blown into our CWA by the high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site reports 3 hours of 1/4SM visibility or less around Warren. Still ongoing at time of criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 14th, a weak disturbance in the mid-levels with strong frontogenesis (FGEN) banding led to widespread wintery conditions across the region. Heavy snow fell across portions of western and central Minnesota where FGEN was most robust. In those areas, snowfall rates increased to 2.0 inches per hour. ||Highest Snowfall Observations include:|7.5 inches of snow 5 miles NW of Hastings, MN. |7.5 inches of snow 5 miles SW of St. Paul, MN. |7.0 inches of snow 1 mile N of New Prague, MN. |7.0 inches of snow 1 mile S of Mahtomedi, MN.|6.9 inches was observed at MSP International Airport (Setting a daily record). |6.5 inches of snow 7 miles SSE of Bird Island, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of heavy snow fell across the southern half of the county. A CoCoRaHS observer reported 6.2 inches of snow fell in Paynesville, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A two-fold event impacted eastern North Dakota and northwestern Minnesota late February. The first prong of the event brought mixed precipitation and some snow, the second brought mesoscale forced snow and wind, with some locations getting up to almost a foot of snow and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions of 1/4SM visibility or less and wind gusts of 35 MPH or more began around 4AM and persisted until around 4PM at the Halstad DOT site. Peak winds were 47mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions of 1/4SM visibility or less and wind gusts of 35 MPH or more began around 3AM and persisted until around 5PM at the Donaldson and Warren DOT sites. Peak winds were 45 MPH and 49 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind an arctic front, a narrow band of light to moderate snowfall and winds gusting from 35 to 55 mph resulted in visibility reductions of one-half mile or less. Travel conditions were briefly quite hazardous, but any blizzard or near blizzard conditions typically lasted for a couple of hours or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility dropped to one-half mile or less for roughly one to two hours due to a combination of snowfall which was generally around one-half inch at Hills and wind gusts as high as 54 mph at a Minnesota Road Weather Information System site near Beaver Creek and 45 mph near Magnolia. Travel conditions were poor during the lowest visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-standing abnormally dry conditions from April 2023 through January 2024 continued through February 2024, persisting severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought in southeast Minnesota. From initial severe drought (D2) issuance in June 2023 to the onset of February 2024, 10 to 20 of total liquid precipitation was 2 to 14 below normal. A minimal number of storms through February provided up to 0.5 of monthly rainfall, 0.5 to 1.25 below normal, and perpetuated the abnormally dry conditions. Record breaking warmth, with most days 10 to 20 degrees above normal, exacerbated dry soil concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than 0.5 of total liquid precipitation fell in Fillmore County through February, near 1.0 below normal, resulting in severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought persisting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of snow provided light accumulations in southeast Minnesota late on February 27th into the early morning of February 28th. While highest overall accumulation reported by volunteer observers was 2.0, accompanying wind gusts near 40 mph resulted in rapid reductions in visibilities as low as 1 mile at times at automated weather observation stations. The combination of strong winds and snow led to snow covered roadways. Multiple accidents were reported in southeast Minnesota through the morning of February 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of light snow and strong wind gusts from 35 to 45 mph resulted in snow covered roadways into the morning hours of February 28th. A light truck travelling west on County Highway 14 slid into the guardrail during the late morning of February 28th resulting in life threatening injuries to the sole occupant driver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration snowfall event affected northeastern Minnesota starting on the 24th of March and continued into the 26th in some areas as multiple waves of snow moved across the region in association with a slow moving area of low pressure. Widespread snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches were reported across the region with higher amounts in excess of 20 inches along the terrain ridge from Duluth to north of Two Harbors and in the Brainerd area. Near blizzard conditions were seen along the North Shore as well with winds up to 30 mph during the heaviest snowfall. While most of the snowfall ended the night of the 25th, lingering light snowfall and continued impacts of poor road conditions lead to travel issues for much of the 26th as well as the region dug out from the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall began during the morning of the 24th and continued into the afternoon of the 25th across Pine County. A dry slot worked into the county and brought an end to snowfall earlier than other nearby areas. Snowfall totals of 8-14 inches were reported with a high report of 16.5 inches near Sandstone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall began during the morning of the 24th and continued through the afternoon of the 26th across southern St. Louis and Carlton counties. Some lake and terrain enhancement was observed in the Duluth area during the event with most of the city seeing anywhere from 14 to 16 inches plus. Snowfall totals of 14-18 inches were reported with a high report of 21 inches along the top of the hill in Duluth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of Koochiching, Itasca, Cass, Crow Wing and Aitkin counties during March. No change in the area of D2 Severe Drought was observed through the month. Precipitation for the month ended up below normal over this area aside from slightly above normal levels in the Brainerd Lakes area. Snowfall was around 2 to 10 inches above normal for the month, but it wasn't enough to lead to any improvement in the noticeably dry conditions present.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted through the month across the southwestern corner of the county. Precipitation for the month was slightly below normal over the western half of the county with slightly above normal precipitation over the eastern half of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large spring storm brought heavy snowfall to much of northeastern Minnesota the 24th into the 26th. Dry air at the surface let to a delayed start across Koochiching County, but snow ended up being a bit heavier than expected when temperatures remained cooler and mixed precipitation did not really occur. Snowfall totals of 7 to 11 inches were reported across the county with a high report of 11.7 inches in International Falls.

While the main portion of the storm ended the evening the 25th, more snow occurred the next day as the storm departed. Snowfall totals of 8 to 12 inches were reported with a high report of 16.4 inches near Sea Gull Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure deepened as it drifted between eastern Nebraska, Iowa and then into Wisconsin before then moving east. This system spread moisture north into west central Minnesota, initially as a rain snow mix before transitioning to accumulating snowfall. A multi-day accumulation of 11 inches was measured by the COOP observer 2 miles east southeast of Artichoke, and 8 inches was observed 1 mile north of Ortonville. In addition to the snowfall, winds across west central Minnesota topped out around 45 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the early afternoon of the 24th and came down heavy for about 6 hours before a break of around 12 hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter storm event through the middle of January ushered in an arctic airmass with bitter cold into Martin Luther King Junior Day. A 20 to 30 degree drop in minimum temperatures from January 13th through January 14th brought minimum temperatures of 10 to 25 degrees below zero across southeast Minnesota. These bitterly cold temperatures lingered into the morning of Martin Luther King Junior Day on January 15th. While sustained winds largely remained calm, occasional gusts pushing 10 to 20 mph at times resulted in wind chills of 25 to 40 degrees below zero. Minimum wind chill reports were -39 degrees in Byron (Dodge County), Rochester (Olmsted County), and Stewartville (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated weather observation station reported a wind chill of 39 degrees below zero in Rochester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second heavy snowfall event concluded a multi-day winter event in southeast Minnesota resulting in a numerous automobile spin offs and accidents. Initial heavier snow bands remained southeast of the area through the early morning hours of January 12th, limiting overall accumulations across southeast Minnesota from 1 to 4. Through the late morning, snowfall bands with rates above 1 per hour wobbled across southeast Minnesota and lasted into the overnight hours. Resultant storm total snowfall accumulations reached 3 to 7 in spots with 7.0 reported in Kasson (Dodge County), Kellogg (Wabasha County), and Winona (Winona County). The combination of increased snowfall rates and wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph resulted in localized whiteouts and rapidly deteriorating travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A volunteer observed reported 6.8 of snowfall near the Town of Houston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minnesota State Patrol reported a fatal automobile collision between a Freightliner M2 Straight freight truck and a Toyota Prius at the intersection of Olmsted County Highway 30 and Olmsted County Highway 8 near 08:45 CST on January 24th. Nearby automated weather observation stations reported visibilities to one eighth of a mile and freezing fog at the estimated time of the collision. Minnesota State Patrol reported wet road conditions at the time of the vehicular accident. This dense fog was long-lived, more intense overnight, from January 23rd into January 29th. While some automated weather observation stations reported visibilities above 1 statute mile at times, predominant hourly visibility observations were less than 1 statute mile during the 5 to 6 day period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large winter storm that churned through the Upper Mississippi River Valley on January 8th provided narrow bands of snowfall rates up to 1 per hour in far southeast Minnesota during the early morning hours of January 9th. While these narrow snowfall bands and accompanying higher accumulations largely remained southeast of the area through January 9th, wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph resulted in snow-covered roadways and multiple slide offs and automobile accidents reported during the morning commute. Highest storm total snowfall accumulation was 6.0 in Caledonia (Houston County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A volunteer observer reported 6.0 of snowfall in Caledonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-standing abnormally dry conditions from April 2023 through January 2024 resulted in no changes to severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought for southeast Minnesota in January. From initial severe drought (D2) issuance in June 2023 to the onset of January 2024, 10 to 20 of total liquid precipitation was 2 to 14 below normal. Multiple storms grazed southeast Minnesota through January and resulted in quite variable snow and rainfall totals. Highest monthly snowfall totals of 10 to 15 with a liquid equivalent of 1 to 1.5 fell along the Mississippi River and were slightly above normal. Lowest monthly snowfall totals from 5 to 10 with up to 1 of total liquid equivalent were near to below normal in counties farther west of the Mississippi River. Besides one week of well below normal temperatures, overall monthly temperatures were well above normal, including multiple days of 10 degrees or more above normal. These warm conditions quickly melted the snowpack and perpetuated ongoing soil moisture concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought persisted for most of Fillmore County through January. While 0.5 to 1.5 of monthly total liquid precipitation was near normal, the long-standing abnormally dry conditions, 8 to 12 below normal since June 1st, 2023, resulted in severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought persisting.

While these narrow snowfall bands and accompanying higher accumulations largely remained southeast of the area through January 9th, wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph resulted in snow-covered roadways and multiple slide offs and automobile accidents reported during the morning commute. Highest storm total snowfall accumulation was 6.0 in Caledonia (Houston County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A volunteer observer reported 1.6 of snowfall in Kellogg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter storm event through the middle of January ushered in an arctic airmass with bitter cold through Martin Luther King Junior Day. A 20 to 30 degree drop in minimum temperatures from January 13th through January 14th brought minimum temperatures of 5 to 15 degrees below zero across southeast Minnesota. Combined with winds gusting 25 to 35 mph, wind chill temperatures reached 30 to 40 degrees below zero during the early morning hours of January 14th across southeast Minnesota. Minimum wind chill reports were -44 degrees in Dexter (Mower County), -43 degrees in Austin (Mower County), -42 degrees in Byron (Dodge County), and -41 degrees in Stewartville (Olmsted County) and Lyle (Mower County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated weather observation station reported a wind chill of 42 degrees below zero in Byron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-standing abnormally dry conditions from April 2023 through January 2024 resulted in no changes to severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought for southeast Minnesota in January. From initial severe drought (D2) issuance in June 2023 to the onset of January 2024, 10 to 20 of total liquid precipitation was 2 to 14 below normal. Multiple storms grazed southeast Minnesota through January and resulted in quite variable snow and rainfall totals. Highest monthly snowfall totals of 10 to 15 with a liquid equivalent of 1 to 1.5 fell along the Mississippi River and were slightly above normal. Lowest monthly snowfall totals from 5 to 10 with up to 1 of total liquid equivalent were near to below normal in counties farther west of the Mississippi River. Besides one week of well below normal temperatures, overall monthly temperatures were well above normal, including multiple days of 10 degrees or more above normal. These warm conditions quickly melted the snowpack and perpetuated ongoing soil moisture concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought persisted for most of Mower County through January as monthly liquid precipitation totals were up to 1 below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm event through the weekend of March 23rd and March 24th provided heavy snowfall in select counties of southeast Minnesota. Initial light snow moved from west to east across the Upper Mississippi River Valley overnight on March 23rd into the early morning hours of March 24th, with a widespread 1 to 2 falling across southeast Minnesota. Highest reported snowfall amounts from volunteer observers during this time were 1.8 in Mantorville (Dodge County), 1.7 in Austin (Mower County), and several reports of 1.5. After a brief respite during the morning hours of March 24th, a heavier snowfall band with 1 per hour snowfall rates impacted southeast Minnesota through the afternoon and evening hours. Widespread snowfall accumulations over a 6 hour period were 2 to 5, with the highest report of 6.0 in Lewiston (Winona County). A slight warming trend through the nighttime hours resulted in an eventual switch to rain early on March 25th. As a result, snowfall totals over the 2 days were quite variable and slightly deflated due to the rain. Overall 2 day snowfall totals were 2 to 6 across southeast Minnesota, highest along and north of the Interstate 90 corridor with 7.9 in Theilman (Wabasha County) and 7.5 in both Grand Meadow (Mower County) and Elgin (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer observers reported 3 to 7 of snowfall in Wabasha County from March 23 through March 24th with the highest amount of 7.2 recorded in Theilman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing abnormally dry conditions slightly eased in southeast Minnesota through March. From initial drought issuance on June 1st, 2023 to March onset, near 15 of total liquid precipitation was more than 9 below normal in drought affected areas in far southeast Minnesota. As a result, persistent extreme (D3) drought affected southeast Minnesota along the Iowa border at the onset of March. Most of the month's total liquid precipitation of 1 to 3 fell in a one week period from March 21st through March 27th. Precipitation totals that week were 2.27 in Austin (Mower County), 2.26 in Rochester (Olmsted County), 2.03 in Winona (Winona County), and 1.86 in Preston (Fillmore County). As a result, the extreme (D2) drought area retreated south but severe (D2) drought conditions persisted in these same areas along the Iowa border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near to slightly below normal monthly total liquid precipitation of 1 to 2 resulted in recession of severe (D2) drought from most of far southern Houston County through March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a hot air balloon striking power lines in Rochester (Olmsted County) on the evening of March 22nd. The Rochester Police Department reported an increased wind gust during an attempted landing resulted in the hot air balloon colliding with power lines near U.S. Highway 63 southbound between the exits for 40th Street Southeast and 48th Street Southeast at 5:50 pm CST. Three occupants of the hot air balloon basket fell 20' to 30' to the ground, with two sustaining minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a hot air balloon accident during the evening hours of March 22nd, striking a power line as it attempted to land in a nearby field. The Rochester Police Department reported a wind gust blew the balloon into power lines. Nearby automated weather observation stations recorded highest sustained winds of 17 miles per hour from the north-northwest and wind gusts of 23 miles one hour before the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure pushed slowly out of the southern Rockies and spurred development of a strong area of low pressure, which moved slolwly from eastern Colorado on the 24th to the western Great Lakes on the 26th. Abundant warm air near the surface and aloft limited wintry impacts over northwest Iowa and forced a mix of wintry types or change to rain or drizzle as far west as the Missouri River in southeast South Dakota as surface temperatures climbed near or above freezing after the initial warm advection snow on the 24th. This coated and crusted over the new snow cover and when intense winds which gusted to 40 to 55 mph developed on the 25th into the 26th behind the system, widespread blizzard conditions failed to develop. Nevertheless, hazardous travel conditions with numerous slide-ins and accidents were reported across much of southeast South Dakota into southwest Minnesota.||Multi-day snow accumulation was generally from 4 to 12 inches from south central to east central South Dakota and into northern areas of southwest Minnesota, with peak wind gusts as high as 55 mph near Wagner, Dallas, and Dixon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early but changed to a prolonged period of rain and mixed precipitation by midday on the 24th as temperatures warmed above freezing. Snow redeveloped by early on the 25th, with a later increase in north winds which gusted as high as 46 mph near Trosky and 36 mph near Pipestone to produce visibility as low as 1/2 mile at times. Snow totaled 5.0 inches at Pipestone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A two-fold event impacted eastern North Dakota and northwestern Minnesota late February. The first prong of the event brought mixed precipitation and some snow, the second brought mesoscale forced snow and wind, with some locations getting up to almost a foot of snow and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warroad 4 W COOP Observer reports 6.7 inches of storm total snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of Koochiching, Itasca, Cass, Crow Wing and Aitkin counties during February. No change in the area of D2 Severe Drought was observed through the month. Precipitation for the month ended up below normal over most of the area aside from slightly above normal levels in the Brainerd Lakes area. Snowfall was around 10 to 20 inches below normal for the month leading to very little snow cover being observed through the month and noticeably dry conditions persisted throughout.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted through the month across the southwestern corner of the county. Precipitation for the month was slightly below normal over most of the county with a tiny area of near to slightly above normal precipitation in the far western reaches of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved northeast from southern Minnesota to near James Bay the 27th into the 28th and brought an area of heavy snowfall to Koochiching County as it passed. Snowfall was lighter elsewhere across the region, but a short period of around 1 per hour snowfall rates allows up to 6 inches of accumulation to fall in a short period of time with 6 inches being reported in Indus by the late afternoon hours of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall fell through the day across Koochiching County as an area of low pressure passed to the south. Snowfall rates of around 1 inch per hour were reported at times and the Indus area received 6 inches of accumulation in a little less than 12 hours before the snow ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow spread across the region on the 12th. As the snow was ending, northwest winds increased and temperatures dropped below zero on the 13th. There was blowing snow across western and central Minnesota, but the lack of snow on the ground kept travel impacts to a minimum. Overnight wind chills fell to near 40 below across western and central Minnesota the night of the 13th into the 14th.||Based on discussions with other forecasters, emergency managers, and Minnesota Department of Transportation, this would have been a high-impact blizzard if there was a deep snow pack.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alexandria airport observed a wind chill of -39 degrees.

A couple automated stations reported maximum wind gusts at or above 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds intermittently peaked between 40 and 45 mph during the afternoon hours. A maximum wind gust of 59 mph was observed at the Minnesota Road Weather Information System site near Florence (MN160).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow spread across the region on the 12th. As the snow was ending, northwest winds increased and temperatures dropped below zero on the 13th. There was blowing snow across western and central Minnesota, but the lack of snow on the ground kept travel impacts to a minimum. Overnight wind chills fell to near 40 below across western and central Minnesota the night of the 13th into the 14th.||Based on discussions with other forecasters, emergency managers, and Minnesota Department of Transportation, this would have been a high-impact blizzard if there was a deep snow pack.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 41 mph at the Morris airport, with blowing snow and reduced visibilities.

Rainfall for the month was near to above normal across the region and rainfall during the first week of the month led to the complete removal of D2 Severe Drought across the area. Total accumulations of 3 to 8 inches led to continuing drought improvement over the course of the month.

Precipitation for the month was above normal over the county and continued drought improvement was observed over the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 20th, isolated severe thunderstorms produced large hail up to half dollar size across southeast Minnesota. Additionally, two brief landspout tornadoes occurred in Dodge county during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported pea to dime sized hail in Byron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northward through the Western Plains during the afternoon and evening of May 6. While instability was not intense with around 1000 J/kg MLCAPE, a very strong low-level jet produced a considerable amount of boundary parallel deep-layer shear to entice a quick linear upscale growth, but the southwest-oriented shear in the 0-3km layer also led to brief mesovortices with enhanced wind corridors in segments which became oriented more northwest to southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds downed several trees around Ivanhoe as well as a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly unstable air mass gained ground through the day near and to the east of an inverted trough which locked in place from central Nebraska into southwest MN. An increase in warm advection into the region along with slightly stronger deep-layer effective shear up to 35 knots allowed for scattered and fairly discrete storms to develop during the evening hours, some of which became supercellular with very large hail. Hail up to 3 inches in diameter fell, with a few storms producing winds in excess of 70 mph.

Heavy rainfall throughout June led to many locations along the Mississippi River in Southeast Minnesota to enter flood stage which persisted into July. During the first week of July many points along the Mississippi River south of Wabasha observed their maximum river stage in minor to moderate flooding. As relatively drier conditions took hold over the first half of July, river stages fell below flood stage into the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant rainfall throughout June pushed the Mississippi River at Lake City into flood stage in June and continued into July. The river stage crested in June and was falling throughout early July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northward through the Western Plains during the afternoon and evening of May 6. While instability was not intense with around 1000 J/kg MLCAPE, a very strong low-level jet produced a considerable amount of boundary parallel deep-layer shear to entice a quick linear upscale growth, but the southwest-oriented shear in the 0-3km layer also led to brief mesovortices with enhanced wind corridors in segments which became oriented more northwest to southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large tree branches were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long standing abnormally dry conditions resulted in severe (D2) drought affecting southernmost parts of Mower, Fillmore, and Houston counties at April onset. Since initial drought issuance near the beginning of July 2023 to the onset of April, 10 to 20 of liquid precipitation was 6 to 10 below normal. Several days of heavy precipitation in April provided 3 to 5 of total monthly liquid precipitation, which was near to slightly above normal in the severe (D2) drought affected affected areas. This brought an end to the severe (D2) drought in southeast Minnesota by late April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near normal monthly rainfall totals alleviated severe (D2) drought along the southern periphery of Mower County towards the end of April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable atmosphere was present in the wake of earlier day convection, as another stronger mid-level wave and associated frontal boundary pushed through southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the evening and early overnight hours. Widespread convection along the boundary later in the evening led to areas of damaging wind and excessive rainfall in east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts up to 58 mph were measured at the Minnesota Road Weather Information System site near Beaver Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 21st, a line of severe storms moved across southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening hours. The storms produced widespread wind damage across the region and one EF-1 tornado in Winona County leading to structural and tree damage in the town of Rollingstone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first widespread heavier snowfall of the season occurred over a two-day period, resulting in 8 to 14 inches of accumulation. The first round of snow began to spread northward across the area very early on January 8 as some areas featured rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour during the morning and midday across southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and western portions of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota. After a brief break, another band of snow developed to the south and wrapped back into northwest Iowa and adjacent areas during the late afternoon and evening hours, again with brief periods of heavier snowfall.||The snow was accompanied by wind gusts of up to 25 to 35 mph, which resulted in some blowing and considerable drifting snow.||Numerous locations set daily snowfall records on either or both January 8th and 9th. Sioux Falls, SD and Sioux City IA set a daily record on January 8, and snowfall over the two-day period ranked in the top 10 for the month of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 9.0 inches at Worthington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multiple day precipitation event from April 1st through April 3rd provided heavy snowfall in parts of southeast Minnesota. Above freezing temperatures resulted in initial rainfall across southeast Minnesota until colder air moved from northwest to southeast late on April 1st, providing a mix of sleet, rain, and freezing rain. These near freezing temperatures persisted into April 3rd in select spots providing the highly variable snowfall accumulations. Storm total accumulations varied from a minimum of 0.3 near Austin (Mower County) to a maximum of 8.0 of Caledonia (Houston County) through April 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer observers reported near 2 of snowfall in Fillmore County with a maximum of 6.0 in Bratsberg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of Koochiching, Itasca, Cass, Crow Wing and Aitkin counties to begin April. Rainfall over the latter half of the month led to a reduction in the area of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 30th leading to the removal of D2 Severe Drought across Crow Wing and Aitkin counties. Precipitation for the month ended up around 0.25-0.75 inches above normal, highest across the southern portion of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across almost all of southern Cass County for most of the month outside of a very small area D1 Moderate Drought along the Crow Wing River in the far southeastern corner of the county. Above normal precipitation during the latter half of the month led to the almost complete removal of D2 Severe Drought across the southern half of the county with the update on the 30th, leaving only a small area just southwest of Backus. Precipitation for the month was around 1 to 2 inches above normal with much of it falling during the last few weeks of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 21st, a line of severe storms moved across southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening hours. The storms produced widespread wind damage across the region and one EF-1 tornado in Winona County leading to structural and tree damage in the town of Rollingstone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fast moving and long track tornado began in St. Charles and produced EF-0 to EF-1 damage to farmsteads and trees in central Winona County. It then moved into Rollingstone, Minnesota were it damaged a few structures and caused widespread tree damage. The tornado continued northeast and crossed the Mississippi River into Buffalo County, Wisconsin where it dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported a four vehicle accident with blowing dust and reduced visibility on Interstate 90 near Austin (Mower County) during the afternoon of April 22nd. Nearby automated weather observation stations reported wind gusts as high as 45 miles per hour through the afternoon hours of April 22nd. Three of the four drivers reported non-life threatening injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A four vehicle accident resulted in three injuries and closure of Interstate 90 at mile marker 183 near Austin. Emergency management reported poor visibility from gusty winds and blowing dust played a role in the accident. Nearby automated weather observation stations reported sustained south-southwest winds as high as 33 miles per hour with gusts up to 45 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system moved through the Upper Mississippi River Valley on April 16th, providing damaging wind gusts across parts of southeast Minnesota. During the afternoon, a line of storms developed across much of the Mississippi River Valley. Automated weather observation stations recorded gusts of 50 to 60 miles per hour as this line progressed through southeast Minnesota. A large semi tractor trailer truck was reported to have blown over during this time near the intersection of Minnesota State Highway 56 and 205th Street in Austin (Mower County). The sole occupant had to be removed from the vehicle and reported non-life threatening injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi tractor trailer truck was reported to be blown over by strong thunderstorm winds near the intersection of Minnesota State Highway 56 and 205th Street in Austin. The driver sustained non-life threatening injuries.

After a brief break, another band of snow developed to the south and wrapped back into northwest Iowa and adjacent areas during the late afternoon and evening hours, again with brief periods of heavier snowfall.||The snow was accompanied by wind gusts of up to 25 to 35 mph, which resulted in some blowing and considerable drifting snow.||Numerous locations set daily snowfall records on either or both January 8th and 9th. Sioux Falls, SD and Sioux City IA set a daily record on January 8, and snowfall over the two-day period ranked in the top 10 for the month of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.3 inches at Windom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Red Flag Conditions consisting of warm weather, dry conditions, and low relative humidity values led to the development of a grass fire near the vicinity of Snake Trail approximately 4 miles NE of Waseca, MN in Waseca County. Less than 2,000 acres burned, and no structural damage occurred. As a precaution, the fire did necessitate the evacuation of multiple residences, and caused three directly related injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grass fire near the vicinity of Snake Trail approximately four miles NE of Waseca, MN approximately at 12 PM and burned for approximately 8 hours. The fire was fully contained at 8 PM. Less than 2,000 acres burned, and no structural damage occurred. As a precaution, the fire did necessitate the evacuation of multiple residences.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal rainfall over the course of June, combined with a robust rainfall event between June 20th and 22nd, led to widespread river flooding across portions of southeast Minnesota. Rainfall amounts upwards of 7 inches between the 20th and 22nd allowed the Mississippi River, Cedar River and many other river basins in southeast Minnesota to enter flood stage towards the end of June. Flooding along the Mississippi River mainstem continued into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant rainfall later into June pushed the Root River at Houston into flood stage on June 23rd. The river stage crested during the late morning on June 24th in minor flood stage at 16.57 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 1.7 inches near Hills.  With winds gusting to around 30 mph, visibility in falling and blowing snow dropped at times to around one mile, creating hazardous travel conditions during the late night and early morning hours of January 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late evening hours on June 2nd, a storm cell passed over portions of Dodge County and produced up to quarter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well above average rainfall throughout June in conjunction with late-month heavy rainfall contributed to additional rises into flood stage along the Mississippi River. The river gauge at Lake City reached minor flood stage before the end of the month and remained in flood stage into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant rainfall throughout June pushed the Mississippi River at Lake City into flood stage on June 27th. The river stage crested during the early evening on June 30th in minor flood stage at 17.26 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours on June 22nd, a slow moving line of thunderstorms produced flooding resulting in several road closures. Most notably Interstate 90 was closed in Mower County due to water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorm generated hail to the size of quarters 13 miles northeast of Ortonville. Heavy rain associated with these storms produced 2.22 inches of moisture measured by the COOP observer in Artichoke Lake. This was on top of 5.83 total inches in May. The heavy rain and runoff from saturated soils produced 20,000 dollars worth of flood related damage across Artichoke Township and 311,656 dollars worth of damage in adjacent Otrey township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 17th, a line of severe storms moved from southwest to northeast through portions of southeast Minnesota. As these storms progressed through the Rochester area, there were reports of both trees down and measured wind gusts of over 60 mph. Storms continued northeast into Wabasha County, producing isolated tree damage before crossing the Mississippi River into Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters in Rochester reported a 62 mph wind gust with a tree down on a powerline as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight surface pressure gradient, combined with a strong low-level jet resulted in high winds across portions of southwest Minnesota on January 21. Winds were maximized along the Buffalo Ridge where isolated gusts above 58 mph were recorded. These winds resulted in some blowing and drifting snow with visibility as low as 1/4 to 1/2 mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds from 40 to 47 mph persisted during the morning and early afternoon of January 21. A peak wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at the Minnesota Department of Transportation site near Lake Benton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day significant heavy rainfall event from June 20-22 resulted in widespread rain totals between 5 and 10 inches across southwest Minnesota. As rainfall reached area streams and rivers, major to record flooding was observed, devastating multiple towns and communities. Extensive overland flooding also resulted in significant impacts and damages. All river crest information is preliminary until made official by the gauge owner.||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was signed for Cottonwood, Jackson, Murray, Nobles, Pipestone, and Rock Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River near Avoca rose above flood stage the morning of June 21st, cresting around 4.64 feet above flood stage (1429.64 feet) on June 24th. This crest is in the Major Stage.  The river stayed above flood stage into July. For a more comprehensive description of flood damage, please see other entries for Murray County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually intense upper-level trough and surface low was located across southern Canada and moved toward the northern Great Lakes with a very deep mixed-layer across the Northern Plains. Synoptic winds gusted mainly 45 to 55 mph, with a few spots reaching around 60 mph in southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong synoptic winds gusted up to 59 mph near Florence, 58 mph at Marshall, and 52 mph at Tracy and Taunton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms erupted late evening on June 16 along a slightly elevated boundary with 2500-3500 J/kg MUCAPE and 30-35 knots of deep-layer effective shear.  Initial storms quickly became widespread along the boundary from south central to east central South Dakota and evolved into a large bow echo, which moved through southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa with numerous reports of damaging winds mainly along and south of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video was taken of multiple trees damaged by a period of damaging thunderstorm wind gusts. Most downed limbs from from 6 to 8 inches in diameter, but several were in excess of a foot in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second mesoscale convective system in as many nights developed across southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota in the late afternoon and early evening in response to a weak mid-level wave and warm advection over a stalled frontal boundary to the south. Rainfall rates up to 2 to 3 inches per hour on top of previously saturated soil resulted in local flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2 to 3 inches in a couple hours on saturated soil resulted in flash flooding. Several roads were inundated near Lake Benton and Verdi. For a more comprehensive description of flood damage, please see other entries for river flooding for Lincoln County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed in a warm air advection regime with 1500 J/kg elevated MUCAPE and 35 knots of effective shear. An isolated stronger cell developed across southwest Minnesota and produced damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four power poles were damaged by thunderstorm winds and resulted in loss of power to substations and a brief power outage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms with very high rainfall rates from 2 to 4 inches per hour developed during the overnight hours June 20-21. The primary focus occurred near and just north of Interstate 90.  The final 24 hour rainfall totals reached 4 to 8 inches by daybreak across Rock County and nearby areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from 5 to 8 inches in the later evening and overnight hours brought widespread flooding to roads in areas to the east and south of Luverne.  One water rescue occurred along Interstate 90 near mile marker 17 when a semi-truck tipped over in the ditch and was mostly covered by water. Interstate 90 was closed near Magnolia for several hours with water flowing over the roadway.  The river gage near Hardwick on the Rock River reached flood stage at 2 am CST after three hours of heavy rainfall in the area. For a more comprehensive description of flood damage, please see other entries for areal and river flooding for Rock County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough and area of low pressure moved into the Northland during the afternoon hours on the 26th. A few thunderstorms developed across the region by late afternoon and brought some small hail to the Duluth area. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure located over northern Minnesota the afternoon of July 7th led to the development of scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms over northeastern Minnesota and northwestern Wisconsin. Most of this activity was very shallow in nature and were not producing hail larger than peas or much in terms of wind gusts. One shower produced a waterspout over Lake Vermilion shortly before 2:30 PM CDT. Waterspouts and funnel clouds were possible across the region due to favorable atmospheric conditions in the vicinity of the low pressure center over the region. This survey was conducted utilizing photos and videos of the waterspout that were posted to social media and through interviews with witnesses. No injuries or fatalities were reported and the waterspout likely remained over open waters of Lake Vermilion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The waterspout developed to the northwest of Echo Point and west of Ely Island on eastern Lake Vermilion. Based on radar and video of the waterspout, it moved slowly to the north-northeast before dissipating near Spider Island. It may have passed near or over Spider Island. It also passed through the vicinity of Taylor and Stonich islands. As of this time, no damage reports have been received from these islands which do have structures on them, so the waterspout may have remained over the water. Based on photos and videos, the waterspout was about 30 yards wide and was on the water for about 2 miles. No fatalities or injuries have been reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms erupted late evening on June 16 along a slightly elevated boundary with 2500-3500 J/kg MUCAPE and 30-35 knots of deep-layer effective shear.  Initial storms quickly became widespread along the boundary from south central to east central South Dakota and evolved into a large bow echo, which moved through southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa with numerous reports of damaging winds mainly along and south of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts reached 60 mph by 0115 CST and peaked at 73 mph at 0117 CST, measured by the Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN007 near Rushmore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center in southeast Nebraska during the morning of May 21 reached southeast Minnesota during the evening. A warm front extended to the east-northeast from the low. Severe storms developed, producing two tornadoes: one in south central MN and another in southeast MN. Severe other storms produced wind damage, mostly to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This wind gust was measured at KFRM airport on the 356 pm CDT observation. The sustained wind was measured at 36 mph. This was likely rear flank downdraft in the wake of the tornado. The previous observation had been at 349 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged area of light snow moved across western Minnesota and produced around 6 inches of snow. The snow began during the morning of January 08, and ended during the early morning hours of January 09. ASOS showed light winds, generally less than 20 mph so blowing snow was not an issue. Temperatures were in the upper teens to upper 20s. A few noteworthy snowfall amounts were:|6.5 inches near St Leo in Yellow Medicine County.|5.3 inches near Montevideo in Chippewa County.|5.0 inches near Bird Island in Renville County.|3.0 inches near Redwood Falls in Redwood County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer measured 5.0 inches near Bird Island in Renville County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system just grazed the area with several hours of light snow. This storm ended up trending farther south east than originally forecast, so snowfall amounts were on the lower side of what was expected. A few noteworthy snow totals were:|6.6 inches near Nerstrand in Goodhue County.|4.0 inches near Albert Lea in Freeborn County.|3.2 inches near Owatonna in Steele County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to 4 inches of snow fell across the county. A total of 3.2 inches was measured near Owatonna in Steele County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered clusters of storms resulted in large hail over portions of east-central Minnesota during the afternoon on July 13. One storm produced a brief tornado in Rice County. A line of storms then developed over northwestern Minnesota during the evening and travelled southeast across central Minnesota throughout the night. This line led to numerous reports of hail and wind damage before it weakened as it moved into Wisconsin.||An unusually warm and humid air mass resulted in heat index values greater than 100F across portions of southern and western Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of July 13. The combination of several thunderstorms and persistent cloud cover kept heat index values in the low to mid 80s further east.

A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills remained -35 degrees or colder at times over a nearly four-day period, with a minimum wind chill of -56 degrees near Rushmore and -52 degrees at Worthington and near Reading during the evening of January 13 and early overnight January 14.

A troughing pattern over the Pacific Northwest ejected a shortwave trough across the Northern Continental U.S. In the mid-levels of the atmosphere, a defined thermal gradient developed over south-central MN which progressed northward into central MN. Forcing for snowfall development was maximized along a narrow band extending from Stearns County to northern Washington County. This forcing led to heavy snowfall rates exceeding one inch per hour with six to eight inches of snow accumulations.||Highest snowfall observations for this event were:|8.5 inches in Lindstrom, MN|8.0 inches 7 miles SE of St. Cloud, MN|7.5 inches in Saint Francis, MN|7.0 inches 5 SW of Isanti, MN|6.0 inches 3 W of Marine on the St. Croix, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to seven inches of snow was reported across the southern region of the county. Seven inches of snow was measured 5 miles SW of Isanti, MN.

This forcing led to heavy snowfall rates exceeding one inch per hour with six to eight inches of snow accumulations.||Highest snowfall observations for this event were:|8.5 inches in Lindstrom, MN|8.0 inches 7 miles SE of St. Cloud, MN|7.5 inches in Saint Francis, MN|7.0 inches 5 SW of Isanti, MN|6.0 inches 3 W of Marine on the St. Croix, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eight inches of snow was reported across the county. Seven and a half inches of snow was measured in Saint Francis, MN.

A large storm system just grazed the area with several hours of light snow. This storm ended up trending farther south east than originally forecast, so snowfall amounts were on the lower side of what was expected. A few noteworthy snow totals were:|6.6 inches near Nerstrand in Goodhue County.|4.0 inches near Albert Lea in Freeborn County.|3.2 inches near Owatonna in Steele County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 2.5 inches at Worthington.  With winds gusting up to 30 to 35 mph, visibility in falling and blowing snow dropped occasionally to below one mile, creating hazardous travel conditions during the morning of January 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the afternoon of May 27, 2024. There was a report of 1 inch hail near Madison Lake in Blue Earth County with one of the strongest storms.
A line of thunderstorms moved across southern Minnesota during the early morning of May 24, 2024. Trees were snapped and the dugout at the baseball field was damaged in Waldorf MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A baseball field dugout was destroyed and a large tree was split. Large branches were also downed. Pictures were relayed via Slack. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful late winter storm swept across the northern plains during late March. Given the time of season, warm surface temperatures near freezing coupled with a conducive snow growth region aloft allowed for heavy wet snow to accumulate quickly across much of Central MN and into a portion of Southern MN. As the low pressure's center reached MN, enhanced forcing aloft allowed for snowfall rates to increase to between 1-2 per hour. ||Areas farther south, closer to the I-90 corridor, observed periods of snow mixed in with rain and ultimately had considerably less snow accumulations as compared to areas farther north. ||Some of the highest snowfall observations included: |17 inches of snow 2 miles S of Mora, MN.|16 inches of snow in Little Falls, MN.|16 inches of snow 7 miles SW of Saint Cloud, MN.|15.7 inches of snow in Braham, MN.|15.3 inches of snow 2 miles NE of Ham Lake, MN.|15.1 inches of snow 5 miles NNE of Big Lake, MN.|14.7 inches of snow 5 miles SE of Stanchfield, MN.|13.8 inches of snow in Albertville, MN.|12.5 inches of snow 6 miles SSE of Rice, MN.|12.2 inches of snow accumulation in Saint Paul, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow moved through and produced generally 8 to 14 inches of heavy wet snow and 35 mph wind gusts. A CoCoRaHS observer measured 14.7 inches of snow 5 miles SE of Stanchfield, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center in southeast Nebraska during the morning of May 21 reached southeast Minnesota during the evening. A warm front extended to the east-northeast from the low. Severe storms developed, producing two tornadoes: one in south central MN and another in southeast MN. Severe other storms produced wind damage, mostly to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado broke about 12-15 trees in three different groves. Two evergreens were also tipped over. It also carved out a path through some tall grasses. The estimated maximum wind speed was 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm impacted central and southern MN the day prior with snow, strong gusty winds, and frigid temperatures. Cold air trailing behind this system remained in place across the region during early pre-dawn hours of January 15th. Wind chills fell to -25 to near -40 degrees below across portions of western MN.  ||The coldest wind chills were observed in extreme western MN near Morris, Glenwood, and Alexandria with -35 to -37 degrees below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS Station located at Myers Field recorded a cold wind chill temperature of -34 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually intense upper-level trough and surface low was located across southern Canada and moved toward the northern Great Lakes with a very deep mixed-layer across the Northern Plains. Synoptic winds gusted mainly 45 to 55 mph, with a few spots reaching around 60 mph in southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong synoptic winds gusted up to 53 mph near Trosky and 50 mph at Pipestone. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day significant heavy rainfall event from June 20-22 resulted in widespread rain totals between 5 and 10 inches across southwest Minnesota. As rainfall reached area streams and rivers, major to record flooding was observed, devastating multiple towns and communities. Extensive overland flooding also resulted in significant impacts and damages. All river crest information is preliminary until made official by the gauge owner.||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was signed for Cottonwood, Jackson, Murray, Nobles, Pipestone, and Rock Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts across Nobles County from June 20-22 ranged from 4 to 10 inches. This rainfall resulted in widespread overland flooding across the county. Okabena Lake in Worthington also overflowed its banks, flooding the downtown area and residential properties surrounding the lake. ||During peak flooding, there were 3 swift water rescues and a reported 6 stalled vehicles. Three apartment buildings and one home in Worthington needed to be evacuated, impacting 35 individuals. County-wide, 350 individuals reported flood damage. Of these, 5 residences were declared destroyed, 55 with major damage, and another 250 with minor damage. ||There were 150 locations that reported flood damage to roads, culverts, and bridges. There were also 40 drainage ditches with major damages. Among numerous township, county, and state roads that were closed and damaged, Interstate 90 was also closed with water over the roadbed in many locations for up to 3 days. Seven of eleven municipalities declared flood damages. The Adrian School District reported water damage to 2 school facilities and there also were collapsed sewer pipes within the city. Preliminary damage assessments estimate loss to public infrastructure to be 3.6 million dollars.

This forcing led to heavy snowfall rates exceeding one inch per hour with six to eight inches of snow accumulations.||Highest snowfall observations for this event were:|8.5 inches in Lindstrom, MN|8.0 inches 7 miles SE of St. Cloud, MN|7.5 inches in Saint Francis, MN|7.0 inches 5 SW of Isanti, MN|6.0 inches 3 W of Marine on the St. Croix, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow was reported across the county. Eight inches of snow was measured 7 miles SE of St. Cloud, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center in southeast Nebraska during the morning of May 21 reached southeast Minnesota during the evening. A warm front extended to the east-northeast from the low. Severe storms developed, producing two tornadoes: one in south central MN and another in southeast MN. Severe other storms produced wind damage, mostly to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were knocked down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo was provided showing a funnel cloud that appeared to be quite close to the ground. However, it was not possible to determine if it ever made ground contact. A broadcast meteorologist surveyed the area and could find no evidence of damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center in southeast Nebraska during the morning of May 21 reached southeast Minnesota during the evening. A warm front extended to the east-northeast from the low. Severe storms developed, producing two tornadoes: one in south central MN and another in southeast MN. Severe other storms produced wind damage, mostly to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on Hall Lake and moved northeast. Numerous photos with locations enabled this to be a virtual survey. The photos are not able to be shared on this site. The beginning of the path was estimated based on photos of the tornado over Hall Lake. Two pontoons were flipped along the shore of Hall Lake. Several dozen trees were broken and a fence was blown down. Near the end of its path, a small metal shed had part of one side blown outward. The estimated maximum wind speed was 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful late winter storm swept across the northern plains during late March. Given the time of season, warm surface temperatures near freezing coupled with a conducive snow growth region aloft allowed for heavy wet snow to accumulate quickly across much of Central MN and into a portion of Southern MN. As the low pressure's center reached MN, enhanced forcing aloft allowed for snowfall rates to increase to between 1-2 per hour. ||Areas farther south, closer to the I-90 corridor, observed periods of snow mixed in with rain and ultimately had considerably less snow accumulations as compared to areas farther north. ||Some of the highest snowfall observations included: |17 inches of snow 2 miles S of Mora, MN.|16 inches of snow in Little Falls, MN.|16 inches of snow 7 miles SW of Saint Cloud, MN.|15.7 inches of snow in Braham, MN.|15.3 inches of snow 2 miles NE of Ham Lake, MN.|15.1 inches of snow 5 miles NNE of Big Lake, MN.|14.7 inches of snow 5 miles SE of Stanchfield, MN.|13.8 inches of snow in Albertville, MN.|12.5 inches of snow 6 miles SSE of Rice, MN.|12.2 inches of snow accumulation in Saint Paul, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow moved through and produced generally 6 to 8 inches of heavy wet snow and 35 mph wind gusts. A CoCoRaHS observer measured 6.5 inches of snow accumulation 4 miles SSW of Milroy, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged area of light snow moved across western Minnesota and produced around 6 inches of snow. The snow began during the morning of January 08, and ended during the early morning hours of January 09. ASOS showed light winds, generally less than 20 mph so blowing snow was not an issue. Temperatures were in the upper teens to upper 20s. A few noteworthy snowfall amounts were:|6.5 inches near St Leo in Yellow Medicine County.|5.3 inches near Montevideo in Chippewa County.|5.0 inches near Bird Island in Renville County.|3.0 inches near Redwood Falls in Redwood County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer measured 5.3 inches near Montevideo in Chippewa County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm impacted central and southern MN the day prior with snow, strong gusty winds, and frigid temperatures. Cold air trailing behind this system remained in place across the region during early pre-dawn hours of January 15th. Wind chills fell to -25 to near -40 degrees below across portions of western MN.  ||The coldest wind chills were observed in extreme western MN near Morris, Glenwood, and Alexandria with -35 to -37 degrees below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS Station located in Olivia recorded a cold wind chill temperature of -34 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills remained -35 degrees or colder at times over a nearly four-day period, with a minimum wind chill of -50 degrees near Lakefield, -49 degrees at Jackson, and -48 degrees at Brewster during the evening of January 13 and early overnight January 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly unstable air mass gained ground through the day near and to the east of an inverted trough which locked in place from central Nebraska into southwest MN. An increase in warm advection into the region along with slightly stronger deep-layer effective shear up to 35 knots allowed for scattered and fairly discrete storms to develop during the evening hours, some of which became supercellular with very large hail. Hail up to 3 inches in diameter fell, with a few storms producing winds in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm impacted central and southern MN the day prior with snow, strong gusty winds, and frigid temperatures. Cold air trailing behind this system remained in place across the region during early pre-dawn hours of January 15th. Wind chills fell to -25 to near -40 degrees below across portions of western MN.  ||The coldest wind chills were observed in extreme western MN near Morris, Glenwood, and Alexandria with -35 to -37 degrees below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS station located at the Benson Municipal Airport recorded a low wind chill of -34 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a cold front during the late morning hours across southwest Minnesota.  The strongest cell produced damaging wind gusts and marginally large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed several trees and powerlines near the intersection of County Roads 15 and 1.  Damage extended further east with minor property damage near the intersection of County Road 15 and 103. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills remained -35 degrees or colder at times over a nearly four-day period, with a minimum wind chill of -56 degrees near Lake Benton, -52 degrees near Hendricks, and -50 degrees at Ivanhoe during the evening of January 13.

A powerful late winter storm swept across the northern plains during late March. Given the time of season, warm surface temperatures near freezing coupled with a conducive snow growth region aloft allowed for heavy wet snow to accumulate quickly across much of Central MN and into a portion of Southern MN. As the low pressure's center reached MN, enhanced forcing aloft allowed for snowfall rates to increase to between 1-2 per hour. ||Areas farther south, closer to the I-90 corridor, observed periods of snow mixed in with rain and ultimately had considerably less snow accumulations as compared to areas farther north. ||Some of the highest snowfall observations included: |17 inches of snow 2 miles S of Mora, MN.|16 inches of snow in Little Falls, MN.|16 inches of snow 7 miles SW of Saint Cloud, MN.|15.7 inches of snow in Braham, MN.|15.3 inches of snow 2 miles NE of Ham Lake, MN.|15.1 inches of snow 5 miles NNE of Big Lake, MN.|14.7 inches of snow 5 miles SE of Stanchfield, MN.|13.8 inches of snow in Albertville, MN.|12.5 inches of snow 6 miles SSE of Rice, MN.|12.2 inches of snow accumulation in Saint Paul, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands consisting of rain mixed with snow moved through and produced generally 1 to 2 inches of snow and 35 mph wind gusts.
