EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered clusters of storms resulted in large hail over portions of east-central Minnesota during the afternoon on July 13. One storm produced a brief tornado in Rice County. A line of storms then developed over northwestern Minnesota during the evening and travelled southeast across central Minnesota throughout the night. This line led to numerous reports of hail and wind damage before it weakened as it moved into Wisconsin.||An unusually warm and humid air mass resulted in heat index values greater than 100F across portions of southern and western Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of July 13. The combination of several thunderstorms and persistent cloud cover kept heat index values in the low to mid 80s further east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chaser located at the intersection 270th St E. and 59th Ave observed a brief tornado to the north-northwest. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through north-central Minnesota in the late afternoon hours on Thursday, September 19, 2024. Out ahead of the cold front in northeast Minnesota, discrete severe thunderstorms began to form in far northeast Minnesota along the US Hwy 53 corridor around 4 PM CT. The first Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued at 4:15 PM for parts of far northeast St. Louis County and northwest Lake County as the severe storm moved northeastward around 30 mph. By 4:55 PM, conditions within a different severe supercell thunderstorm warranted the first Tornado Warning of the evening to be issued for central St. Louis County around the communities of Cotton and Canyon, MN. Between 5 and 5:30 PM that evening, multiple additional Severe Thunderstorm and Tornado Warnings were issued along and east of US Hwy 53 and along the I-35 corridor into east- central Minnesota. The cold front continued to move eastward through the early evening hours on the 19th, with storms beginning to enter Douglas and Burnett Counties in northwest Wisconsin towards 6 PM as low-level funnel clouds were reported by law enforcement and trained weather spotters. Hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter was reported from the strongest thunderstorms, but in very localized reports. As the air mass began to cool and stabilized in the mid-evening hours, the threat for additional tornadoes began to wane and the strongest thunderstorms were limited to the Twin Ports and across northwest Wisconsin. As the cold front entered northwest Wisconsin by late evening, activity subsided to only showers and general thunderstorms with no additional impacts reported overnight. In total, eight Tornado Warnings, nine Severe Thunderstorms Warnings and two Special Marine Warnings were issued for this severe weather event. The Cotton/Canyon Tornado is the only confirmed tornado from this severe weather event at this time. While funnel clouds were reported with several other storms, there is currently no evidence that any of the other storms produced a tornado that reached the ground and produced damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm formed halfway between Meadowlands and Canyon, MN, between 4:35 to 4:40 PM on Thursday, September 19, 2024. As the strong thunderstorm continued to show potential for producing a tornado, the National Weather Service office in Duluth, MN, issued a Severe Thunderstorm Warning at 4:44 PM for this storm. The strong storm moved eastward over unpopulated marshes immediately north of Lake Nichols Road about 4 miles north-northwest of Canyon, MN, by 5:00 PM. Meteorologists at the National Weather Service issued a Tornado Warning for this storm at 4:55 PM as early signs of tornadic development appeared on radar, and a short time later a tornado was confirmed by trained spotters and law enforcement. Visible tree damage from the tornado, estimated to have begun at 5:05 PM CDT, was first observed on the west side of US Highway 53 about 4.5 miles south of Cotton, MN, with pine trees snapped at the base. The tornado continued to move northeastward to uproot trees along Three Lakes Road about one-half mile east of the U.S. Highway 53 intersection. The storm began to move more north-northeastward so the tornado track shifted more northward with the most widespread surveyed damage seen along Bug Creek Road about 2.5 miles east-southeast of Cotton, MN. Over 50 pine trees and numerous clusters of aspen trees were observed to be snapped at the base and/or uprooted, estimated to have occurred at around 5:15 PM. A few still-standing pine trees had metal sheets lofted and wrapped around them. Minor to moderate structure damage was reported by two property owners along Bug Creek Road before the tornado passed northward over the Whiteface River. Towards 5:18 PM, the tornado began to weaken and finally lifted off the ground. The last uprooted trees, 4 aspen, were last observed at the intersection of Comstock Lake Road and Melrude Road, 2.7 miles east of Cotton, MN. In total, the tornado was on the ground for a total of 5.9 miles and for around 13 minutes. The damage rating from the NWS Duluth Storm Survey team resulted in an EF-1 rating with winds estimated at 105 mph. There were a total of two Tornado Warning in effect from 4:55 PM to 5:45 PM for this tornadic supercell thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged area of light snow moved across western Minnesota and produced around 6 inches of snow. The snow began during the morning of January 08, and ended during the early morning hours of January 09. ASOS showed light winds, generally less than 20 mph so blowing snow was not an issue. Temperatures were in the upper teens to upper 20s. A few noteworthy snowfall amounts were:|6.5 inches near St Leo in Yellow Medicine County.|5.3 inches near Montevideo in Chippewa County.|5.0 inches near Bird Island in Renville County.|3.0 inches near Redwood Falls in Redwood County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 5.3 and 5.0 inches were measured in the eastern half of the county near Montevideo. Snowfall was higher to the west, and there was a 6.5 inch report just south of the county line in Yellow Medicine county. Extrapolating this northward supports at least 6 inches of snow in western or central Lac Qui Parle county.

Thunderstorms erupted late evening on June 16 along a slightly elevated boundary with 2500-3500 J/kg MUCAPE and 30-35 knots of deep-layer effective shear.  Initial storms quickly became widespread along the boundary from south central to east central South Dakota and evolved into a large bow echo, which moved through southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa with numerous reports of damaging winds mainly along and south of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo was provided of some significant tree damage on the edge of Ellsworth with up to one foot diameter tree limbs snapped off. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated convection along a warm front caused severe winds, hail, and flash flooding in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail reported by the public 6 miles SSE of Florian, with estimated 50 mph winds along with the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills remained -35 degrees or colder at times over a nearly four-day period, with a minimum wind chill of -54 degrees near Florence, -51 degrees near Balaton, -47 degrees at Tracy, and -45 degrees at Marshall during the evening of January 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful late winter storm swept across the northern plains during late March. Given the time of season, warm surface temperatures near freezing coupled with a conducive snow growth region aloft allowed for heavy wet snow to accumulate quickly across much of Central MN and into a portion of Southern MN. As the low pressure's center reached MN, enhanced forcing aloft allowed for snowfall rates to increase to between 1-2 per hour. ||Areas farther south, closer to the I-90 corridor, observed periods of snow mixed in with rain and ultimately had considerably less snow accumulations as compared to areas farther north. ||Some of the highest snowfall observations included: |17 inches of snow 2 miles S of Mora, MN.|16 inches of snow in Little Falls, MN.|16 inches of snow 7 miles SW of Saint Cloud, MN.|15.7 inches of snow in Braham, MN.|15.3 inches of snow 2 miles NE of Ham Lake, MN.|15.1 inches of snow 5 miles NNE of Big Lake, MN.|14.7 inches of snow 5 miles SE of Stanchfield, MN.|13.8 inches of snow in Albertville, MN.|12.5 inches of snow 6 miles SSE of Rice, MN.|12.2 inches of snow accumulation in Saint Paul, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow moved through and produced generally 6 to 8 inches of heavy wet snow and 35 mph wind gusts. A CoCoRaHS observer measured 8.1 inches of snow accumulation in Lester Prairie, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered clusters of storms resulted in large hail over portions of east-central Minnesota during the afternoon on July 13. One storm produced a brief tornado in Rice County. A line of storms then developed over northwestern Minnesota during the evening and travelled southeast across central Minnesota throughout the night. This line led to numerous reports of hail and wind damage before it weakened as it moved into Wisconsin.||An unusually warm and humid air mass resulted in heat index values greater than 100F across portions of southern and western Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of July 13. The combination of several thunderstorms and persistent cloud cover kept heat index values in the low to mid 80s further east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down in St. Augusta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a cold front during the late morning hours across southwest Minnesota.  The strongest cell produced damaging wind gusts and marginally large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail stripped leaves from trees. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually intense upper-level trough and surface low was located across southern Canada and moved toward the northern Great Lakes with a very deep mixed-layer across the Northern Plains. Synoptic winds gusted mainly 45 to 55 mph, with a few spots reaching around 60 mph in southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong synoptic winds gusted up to 55 mph near Beaver Creek and 52 mph at Luverne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through north-central Minnesota in the late afternoon hours on Thursday, September 19, 2024. Out ahead of the cold front in northeast Minnesota, discrete severe thunderstorms began to form in far northeast Minnesota along the US Hwy 53 corridor around 4 PM CT. The first Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued at 4:15 PM for parts of far northeast St. Louis County and northwest Lake County as the severe storm moved northeastward around 30 mph. By 4:55 PM, conditions within a different severe supercell thunderstorm warranted the first Tornado Warning of the evening to be issued for central St. Louis County around the communities of Cotton and Canyon, MN. Between 5 and 5:30 PM that evening, multiple additional Severe Thunderstorm and Tornado Warnings were issued along and east of US Hwy 53 and along the I-35 corridor into east- central Minnesota. The cold front continued to move eastward through the early evening hours on the 19th, with storms beginning to enter Douglas and Burnett Counties in northwest Wisconsin towards 6 PM as low-level funnel clouds were reported by law enforcement and trained weather spotters. Hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter was reported from the strongest thunderstorms, but in very localized reports. As the air mass began to cool and stabilized in the mid-evening hours, the threat for additional tornadoes began to wane and the strongest thunderstorms were limited to the Twin Ports and across northwest Wisconsin. As the cold front entered northwest Wisconsin by late evening, activity subsided to only showers and general thunderstorms with no additional impacts reported overnight. In total, eight Tornado Warnings, nine Severe Thunderstorms Warnings and two Special Marine Warnings were issued for this severe weather event. The Cotton/Canyon Tornado is the only confirmed tornado from this severe weather event at this time. While funnel clouds were reported with several other storms, there is currently no evidence that any of the other storms produced a tornado that reached the ground and produced damage.

As the cold front entered northwest Wisconsin by late evening, activity subsided to only showers and general thunderstorms with no additional impacts reported overnight. In total, eight Tornado Warnings, nine Severe Thunderstorms Warnings and two Special Marine Warnings were issued for this severe weather event. The Cotton/Canyon Tornado is the only confirmed tornado from this severe weather event at this time. While funnel clouds were reported with several other storms, there is currently no evidence that any of the other storms produced a tornado that reached the ground and produced damage.

A cold front moved through north-central Minnesota in the late afternoon hours on Thursday, September 19, 2024. Out ahead of the cold front in northeast Minnesota, discrete severe thunderstorms began to form in far northeast Minnesota along the US Hwy 53 corridor around 4 PM CT. The first Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued at 4:15 PM for parts of far northeast St. Louis County and northwest Lake County as the severe storm moved northeastward around 30 mph. By 4:55 PM, conditions within a different severe supercell thunderstorm warranted the first Tornado Warning of the evening to be issued for central St. Louis County around the communities of Cotton and Canyon, MN. Between 5 and 5:30 PM that evening, multiple additional Severe Thunderstorm and Tornado Warnings were issued along and east of US Hwy 53 and along the I-35 corridor into east- central Minnesota. The cold front continued to move eastward through the early evening hours on the 19th, with storms beginning to enter Douglas and Burnett Counties in northwest Wisconsin towards 6 PM as low-level funnel clouds were reported by law enforcement and trained weather spotters. Hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter was reported from the strongest thunderstorms, but in very localized reports. As the air mass began to cool and stabilized in the mid-evening hours, the threat for additional tornadoes began to wane and the strongest thunderstorms were limited to the Twin Ports and across northwest Wisconsin. As the cold front entered northwest Wisconsin by late evening, activity subsided to only showers and general thunderstorms with no additional impacts reported overnight. In total, eight Tornado Warnings, nine Severe Thunderstorms Warnings and two Special Marine Warnings were issued for this severe weather event. The Cotton/Canyon Tornado is the only confirmed tornado from this severe weather event at this time. While funnel clouds were reported with several other storms, there is currently no evidence that any of the other storms produced a tornado that reached the ground and produced damage.

A well-defined cold front associated with a strong upper-level low produced widespread wind damage and a few tornadoes throughout central and eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin on August 29th, 2024. Four tornadoes occurred. There were also a number of downbursts, with some of the most significant downburst damage in Champlin, Anoka, and Cottage Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure locked in very warm and extremely humid conditions. Dew points in the upper 70s and lower 80s combined with temperatures in the upper 80s to lower 90s to produce daily heat index values from 100 to 120 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and early evening heat index values reached as high as 114 degrees near Hardwick and 107 degrees at Luverne on August 25, as well as 109 degrees near Beaver Creek and 108 degrees at Magnolia on August 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Colorado Low tracked northeast through NE Iowa / SE Minnesota / Wisconsin, bringing a period of rain that changed over to snow across portions of south and east-central Minnesota. The snow was heavy at times, with visibilities dropping to a quarter mile or less at times. Despite warm ground temperatures, snow was able to accumulate up to 5 inches thanks to dynamic cooling associated with strong lift ahead of the upper level low.||Highest snowfall observations for this event were:|5.2 inches 3.8 miles NNW of Howard Lake, MN|4.5 inches 4.3 miles NNE of Maple Lake, MN|4.3 inches in Dassel, MN|4.3 inches 3 miles NNW of Plymouth, MN|4.3 inches 3 miles W of Monticello, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of 2 to 4 inches fell across the western part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas ahead of a weak frontal boundary exhibited pooling of moisture with dew point readings into the upper 70s to lower 80s. As temperatures reached the lower to mid 90s, dangerous heat conditions developed during the afternoon hours with heat index values from 100 to 111 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Afternoon heat index values reached 101 degrees at a personal weather station near Hardwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pronounced cold front sagged south of Canada, colliding with a warm and humid air mass across central Minnesota. Surface temperatures reached the upper 80s/lower 90s with dew points in the low to mid 70s. This created an unstable environment conducive for storms supporting large hail, strong winds, and a few tornadoes. ||A pair of strong supercells formed along the front over central Minnesota during the late afternoon and travelled south-southeast through the evening. Initial storms produced very large hail up to 3 inches in diameter, with sporadic reports of wind damage. An EF-1 tornado impacted Melrose, MN as the supercells travelled through Stearns County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado moved south-southeast through the west side of Melrose. The worst structural damage took place in the northwest side of town. The tornado destroyed a large metal building system and peeled the roof off of several other buildings. Portions of the roof were torn off some townhomes. Hundreds of trees were uprooted or broken. The tornado moved across a golf course, taking down many dozens of trees. In the far southern part of Melrose, more homes sustained damage and the tornado completely destroyed a large machine shed at a farm. After exiting town, the tornado moved across fields and damaged a few more farms. One turkey barn had much of its roof removed and the roof was blown off a large shed shortly before the tornado dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought resurfaced in the state of Minnesota in October, with 11 counties experiencing D2 drought in at least some areas of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hubbard county experienced 4 weeks of D2 drought in October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of minimal precipitation across southeast Minnesota from the beginning of September and throughout much of October resulted in rainfall deficits of 4 to 7 inches below normal. As a result, severe drought conditions developed at the end of October in northeast Iowa. Severe drought conditions persisted through October 30th when a substantial rainfall event brought 0.5 to 1.5 inches of rainfall across northeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Throughout the entirety of September and through October 29th, only 1 to 2 inches of precipitation fell in Fillmore County. This 4 to 6 inch deficit for this period resulted in the development of short-lived severe drought conditions by late October. Severe drought conditions ended in the county by the end of the month when roughly 0.75 to 2 inches of rainfall fell across the county on October 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Colorado Low tracked northeast through NE Iowa / SE Minnesota / Wisconsin, bringing a period of rain that changed over to snow across portions of south and east-central Minnesota. Despite warm ground temperatures, snow was able to accumulate up to 5 inches thanks to dynamic cooling associated with strong lift ahead of the upper level low.||Highest snowfall observations for this event were:|5.2 inches 3.8 miles NNW of Howard Lake, MN|4.5 inches 4.3 miles NNE of Maple Lake, MN|4.3 inches in Dassel, MN|4.3 inches 3 miles NNW of Plymouth, MN|4.3 inches 3 miles W of Monticello, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of 2 to 4 inches fell across the county.  4 inches of snow were measured 2.1 miles NW of Hutchinson, MN and 0.3 miles NE of Plato, MN.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported water inside of a parked vehicle on the east side of Winona.

This line led to numerous reports of hail and wind damage before it weakened as it moved into Wisconsin.||An unusually warm and humid air mass resulted in heat index values greater than 100F across portions of southern and western Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of July 13. The combination of several thunderstorms and persistent cloud cover kept heat index values in the low to mid 80s further east.

Despite warm ground temperatures, snow was able to accumulate up to 5 inches thanks to dynamic cooling associated with strong lift ahead of the upper level low.||Highest snowfall observations for this event were:|5.2 inches 3.8 miles NNW of Howard Lake, MN|4.5 inches 4.3 miles NNE of Maple Lake, MN|4.3 inches in Dassel, MN|4.3 inches 3 miles NNW of Plymouth, MN|4.3 inches 3 miles W of Monticello, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of 2 to 4 inches fell across the eastern part of the county.  3.3 inches of snow was measured 2.1 miles S of Saint Augusta, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front was draped across southern Minnesota on the afternoon of Monday, August 5, 2024.  A very unstable airmass with strong shear was present along and south of the front.  Two supercells developed over Nicollet and Watonwan Counties, then strengthened while tracking southeast.  Supercells eventually formed a line of severe thunderstorms, which continued to result in occasional tornadoes and damaging wind gusts across south central MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado mostly moved across fields, but it did hit one house. A storm chaser took video of the tornado. Satellite imagery showed a path through the crops, enabling a determination of the beginning and end points.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front was draped across southern Minnesota on the afternoon of Monday, August 5, 2024.  A very unstable airmass with strong shear was present along and south of the front.  Two supercells developed over Nicollet and Watonwan Counties, then strengthened while tracking southeast.  Supercells eventually formed a line of severe thunderstorms, which continued to result in occasional tornadoes and damaging wind gusts across south central MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado mostly moved across corn and bean fields, with non-continuous crop damage. It did move across a farmstead where it uprooted numerous trees, but there was little damage to nearby structures. A power pole was damaged. Several storm chasers took video of the tornado, including one who filmed it for nearly seven minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-defined cold front associated with a strong upper-level low produced widespread wind damage and a few tornadoes throughout central and eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin on August 29th, 2024. Four tornadoes occurred. There were also a number of downbursts, with some of the most significant downburst damage in Champlin, Anoka, and Cottage Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed at the southern edge of Bellwood Oaks Golf Course, uprooting several trees, then continued northeast. A narrow path of sporadic tree damage occurred along the track, with the last damage in Minnesota being on Blackbird Trail in Dakota County. The tornado tracked across a sliver of northern Goodhue County before crossing the Mississippi River into Wisconsin, dissipating about 5 miles southeast of Prescott in Pierce County.

Four tornadoes occurred. There were also a number of downbursts, with some of the most significant downburst damage in Champlin, Anoka, and Cottage Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed at the southern edge of Bellwood Oaks Golf Course, in Dakota County. It moved northeast and tracked across a sliver of northern Goodhue County before crossing the Mississippi River into Wisconsin, dissipating about 5 miles southeast of Prescott in Pierce County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-defined cold front associated with a strong upper-level low produced widespread wind damage and a few tornadoes throughout central and eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin on August 29th, 2024. Four tornadoes occurred. There were also a number of downbursts, with some of the most significant downburst damage in Champlin, Anoka, and Cottage Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado hit several farms before moving into Lastrup and dissipating. It ripped most of the roof off of two barns west and northwest of Pierz, then continued northeast to Lastrup, where it knocked down or broke large limbs of dozens of trees and tore off half of the roof of an outbuilding. Drone video from the Sheriff's Office showed a convergent damage pattern in Lastrup, and the damage survey showed a convergent pattern at several locations before the tornado hit Lastrup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cooler air wrapped into a low pressure system, which developed along a slow moving frontal boundary across Iowa and Minnesota early on October 31.  Rain changed to snowfall, which accumulated mainly at locations with slightly higher elevation.  With winds gusting to 25 to 35 mph, there were brief periods of visibility less than a mile with slick conditions resulting in numerous traffic accidents on secondary roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light to occasionally moderate snowfall totaled 2.7 inches at Windom and 2.0 inches at Delft. Northwest winds gusted from 25 to 35 mph during snowfall and resulted in reduced visibility as low as 1/2 to 1 mile at times of greater snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cooler air wrapped into a low pressure system, which developed along a slow moving frontal boundary across Iowa and Minnesota early on October 31. With winds gusting to 25 to 35 mph, there were brief periods of visibility less than a mile with slick conditions resulting in numerous traffic accidents on secondary roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light to occasionally moderate snowfall totaled 1.0 inch at Worthington. Northwest winds gusted from 25 to 35 mph during snowfall and resulted in reduced visibility as low as 1 mile at times of greater snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-defined cold front associated with a strong upper-level low produced widespread wind damage and a few tornadoes throughout central and eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin on August 29th, 2024. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms developed across western Minnesota and moved northeast into the Brainerd Lakes area during the early to mid-afternoon of the 29th ahead of a cold front. The storms quickly began to weaken as the pushed across the Brainerd Lakes area as they encountered a more stable airmass across northeastern Minnesota. Only a few sporadic reports of damage were received, but one property lost most of the trees on the site.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were snapped about 5 feet up from the base. Observer noted they had damage to most of the trees on their property.

After a relatively mild summer, a period of excessive heat impacted much of the region prompting air temperatures to reach well above 90 degrees during the afternoon on August 26th. This combined with dewpoints as high as 80 degrees in some locations allowed for heat indices to climb above 110 degrees on August 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anomalously warm air temperatures and high humidity allowed the maximum heat index to reach between 104 to 119 degrees on August 26th across Fillmore County. Heat index values reached 119 degrees at the automated weather observation station at Preston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms formed along a strong cold front during the early afternoon across southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. A stronger storm briefly produced hail up to quarter size, but failed to remain discrete enough to maintain strength.

Areas ahead of a weak frontal boundary exhibited pooling of moisture with dew point readings into the upper 70s to lower 80s. As temperatures reached the lower to mid 90s, dangerous heat conditions developed during the afternoon hours with heat index values from 100 to 111 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Afternoon heat index values reached 102 degrees at a personal weather station near Round Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure locked in very warm and extremely humid conditions. Dew points in the upper 70s and lower 80s combined with temperatures in the upper 80s to lower 90s to produce daily heat index values from 100 to 120 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and early evening heat index values reached as high as 117 degrees near Lakefield and 110 degrees at Jackson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered clusters of storms resulted in large hail over portions of east-central Minnesota during the afternoon on July 13. One storm produced a brief tornado in Rice County. A line of storms then developed over northwestern Minnesota during the evening and travelled southeast across central Minnesota throughout the night. This line led to numerous reports of hail and wind damage before it weakened as it moved into Wisconsin.||An unusually warm and humid air mass resulted in heat index values greater than 100F across portions of southern and western Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of July 13. The combination of several thunderstorms and persistent cloud cover kept heat index values in the low to mid 80s further east.

An early morning line of showers and thunderstorms intensified during the afternoon upon encountering a favorable environment for severe thunderstorms. Steep mid-level lapse rates combined with strong instability. The storms produced damaging wind gusts which downed trees in several areas and mostly quarter to hen egg size hail, but some hail was much larger.||A hailstone in Stevens County measured six inches in diameter (it was photographed next to a ruler). This at least tied the record for the largest hailstone in Minnesota. Storm Data records also indicate six inch diameter hail in Nobles County on July 28, 1986, and in Murray County on July 4, 1968. The hail was collected about 13 to 15 minutes after the hail ended but was not saved in a freezer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree branch was down. A photo was provided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of central St. Louis, northern Itasca, Cass and Crow Wing counties during December. Aside from a reduction in the area of D2 Severe Drought across central St. Louis County with the update on the 17th, no other changes in D2 Severe Drought were observed over the course of the month. Precipitation for the month was generally within a half inch both above and below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: December began with D2 Severe Drought conditions across much of the northern half of central St. Louis County. Precipitation during the beginning of the month led to the reduction of D2 Severe Drought, leaving just a small area along the Itasca County border with the update on the 17th. No further changes in the area of D2 Severe Drought were observed over the remainder of the month. Precipitation for December was slightly above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late evening hours of December 18th, a surface low pressure system began to move southeast out of the Dakotas. The system, originally characterized as a clipper due to its northwest to southeast motion, was unique in that it was ripe with Pacific moisture. Accumulating snow started during the early morning hours on December 19th, with most of the snow falling during the morning commute. A few deformation bands of light snow continued through the evening of December 19th.The end result was a widespread swath of 5-8��� of snow across south central Minnesota. ||Some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|8.9 inches of snowfall in Faribault, MN (Rice)|8.3 inches of snowfall 1 mile west of Elko New Market, MN (Scott)|8.0 inches of snowfall 5 miles north-northwest of Le Center (Le Sueur) |7.9 inches of snowfall in Henderson, MN (Sibley)|7.5 inches of snowfall in Excelsior, MN (Hennepin)|7.2 inches of snowfall in Oakdale, MN (Washington)|7.1 inches of snowfall in Welch, MN (Goodhue).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interpolated snowfall analysis from CoCoRaHS reports showed three to five inches accumulation across the county.

On the morning of July 21st, a cluster of showers and thunderstorms containing heavy rainfall developed over the Twin Cities metropolitan area along a slow-moving surface frontal boundary. Rainfall rates neared two inches per hour which caused urban flash flooding in downtown St. Paul at the intersection of I-35E and Shepard Rd. A car was left stranded with all lanes of traffic underwater.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A MNDOT traffic camera showed the intersection of I-35E and Shepard Rd flooded, with one car stranded in the flood water due to heavy rainfall. Other cars were using the freeway entrance ramp to get around the flooded roadway since the regular traffic lanes were all under water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were snapped, four miles north of Ft. Snelling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure aloft over the Great Lakes featured a southward moving surface cold front located across central MN and western WI. A line of thunderstorms developed during the evening of July 22nd and produced strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser measured the wind gust on a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure aloft over the Great Lakes featured a southward moving surface cold front located across central MN and western WI. A line of thunderstorms developed during the evening of July 22nd and produced strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and several branches up to 12 inches in diameter were downed onto parked cars in downtown Chaska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a stationary surface boundary which was across northern MN to eastern SD. Most of the thunderstorms were sub-severe, however a few did produce periods of heavy rainfall and strong wind gusts. A few trees were downed in Yellow Medicine and Wright Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down, blocking 10th St. between Keats Ave and County Road 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of freezing drizzle, ice/snow pellets, and rain during the early morning hours transitioned to snow showers by late morning. Convective bands of heavier snow formed, leading to narrow corridors of dramatically reduced visibilities and 0.5 to 1 inch per hour snowfall rates. Northwest winds were also gusting up to 45 mph in eastern MN, and up to 50 mph in west-central MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick burst of heavy snow brought a band of up to 0.5 inches, and reduced visibilities to 1/4 mile near I-94 and US HWY 59.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of strong thunderstorms across southwestern MN was located on the eastern periphery of an unstable air mass. A subtle shortwave over the northern plains and a strong low-level jet supported a large hail and damaging wind threat. Strong 60-80 mph winds brought down trees and power lines across Kandiyohi, Chippewa, Renville Counties, including overturning a semi in Hwy 29. A brief tornado occurred north of Clara City. Quarter to Golf Ball sized hail was also reported with these thunderstorms.

A cluster of strong thunderstorms across southwestern MN was located on the eastern periphery of an unstable air mass. A subtle shortwave over the northern plains and a strong low-level jet supported a large hail and damaging wind threat. Strong 60-80 mph winds brought down trees and power lines across Kandiyohi, Chippewa, Renville Counties, including overturning a semi in Hwy 29. A brief tornado occurred north of Clara City. Quarter to Golf Ball sized hail was also reported with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi was blown off Highway 29 just north of the Chippewa/Swift County line.

Areas of freezing drizzle, ice/snow pellets, and rain during the early morning hours transitioned to snow showers by late morning. Convective bands of heavier snow formed, leading to narrow corridors of dramatically reduced visibilities and 0.5 to 1 inch per hour snowfall rates. Northwest winds were also gusting up to 45 mph in eastern MN, and up to 50 mph in west-central MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick burst of heavy snow brought a band of up to 0.5 inches, and reduced visibilities to 1/4 mile near I-94 and US HWY 59.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of central St. Louis, northern Itasca, Cass and Crow Wing counties during December. Aside from a reduction in the area of D2 Severe Drought across central St. Louis County with the update on the 17th, no other changes in D2 Severe Drought were observed over the course of the month. Precipitation for the month was generally within a half inch both above and below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very small area of D2 Severe Drought persisted through the month of December in the far northwestern corner of the county. D1 Moderate Drought covered most of the county with a small area of D0 Abnormally Dry conditions in the far southern reaches of the county. No changes in drought status were observed over the course of the month. Precipitation for the month was near to slightly below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered clusters of storms resulted in large hail over portions of east-central Minnesota during the afternoon on July 13. One storm produced a brief tornado in Rice County. A line of storms then developed over northwestern Minnesota during the evening and travelled southeast across central Minnesota throughout the night. This line led to numerous reports of hail and wind damage before it weakened as it moved into Wisconsin.||An unusually warm and humid air mass resulted in heat index values greater than 100F across portions of southern and western Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of July 13. The combination of several thunderstorms and persistent cloud cover kept heat index values in the low to mid 80s further east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down in Annandale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning line of showers and thunderstorms intensified during the afternoon upon encountering a favorable environment for severe thunderstorms. Steep mid-level lapse rates combined with strong instability. The storms produced damaging wind gusts which downed trees in several areas and mostly quarter to hen egg size hail, but some hail was much larger.||A hailstone in Stevens County measured six inches in diameter (it was photographed next to a ruler). This at least tied the record for the largest hailstone in Minnesota. Storm Data records also indicate six inch diameter hail in Nobles County on July 28, 1986, and in Murray County on July 4, 1968. The hail was collected about 13 to 15 minutes after the hail ended but was not saved in a freezer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported numerous 3 inch diameter branches down as well as a 10 inch diameter pine tree that was uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure aloft over the Great Lakes featured a southward moving surface cold front located across central MN and western WI. A line of thunderstorms developed during the evening of July 22nd and produced strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a stationary surface boundary which was across northern MN to eastern SD. Most of the thunderstorms were sub-severe, however a few did produce periods of heavy rainfall and strong wind gusts. A few trees were downed in Yellow Medicine and Wright Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of southwest Minnesota were on the southern edge of a band of snowfall which brushed areas north of Interstate 90, and was heaviest from 3 to 6 inches north of Highway 30. A majority of poor visibility conditions as low as one mile or less was caused by the snowfall which was largely ahead of the stronger winds with this system. Winds were strongest toward the South Dakota and Iowa borders with gusts as high as 35 to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained at times up to 36 mph at Rushmore and 33 mph at Worthington.

During the afternoon and evening hours on December 5th, a strong arctic cold front swept across the region bringing wind gusts to southeast Minnesota as high as 62 mph at Rochester International Airport. When encountering strong winds, a semi-trailer in Fillmore County was blown over resulting in two non-life threatening injuries to the occupants.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 35 to 45 mph persisted across much of Fillmore County during the afternoon and early evening hours on December 4th with a peak measured non-thunderstorm wind gust at Preston Airport of 58 mph. Additionally, a semi-trailer was blown over after encountering strong winds near Harmony resulting in two non-life threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 19th, a potent winter system brought heavy snow amounts as high as 5 to 8 inches in a narrow band near the Interstate 90 corridor beginning in the morning hours and continuing through the overnight. In Minnesota, two separate traffic accidents occurred that resulted in three non-life threatening injuries while snow was ongoing, with snow covered roads observed by Minnesota State Patrol. The system progressed east into portions of western Wisconsin where additional heavy snow totals were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across Winona County ranged from 4 and 8 inches as a result of this system. The highest storm total snow accumulation in the county of 8.1 was reported 4 miles southeast of Minneiska.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across Olmsted County ranged between 5 and 8 inches as a result of this system. The highest storm total snow accumulation in the county of 8.0 was reported 3 miles northeast of Rochester. Additionally, two separate vehicle accidents resulting in 3 non-life threatening injuries occurred in Olmsted County while snow was ongoing.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 35 to 45 mph persisted across Olmsted County for much of the afternoon and early evening hours on December 4th. Additionally, the ASOS at Rochester International Airport observed a peak 62 mph non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 19th, a potent winter system brought heavy snow amounts as high as 5 to 8 inches in a narrow band near the Interstate 90 corridor beginning in the morning hours and continuing through the overnight. In Minnesota, two separate traffic accidents occurred that resulted in three non-life threatening injuries while snow was ongoing, with snow covered roads observed by Minnesota State Patrol. The system progressed east into portions of western Wisconsin where additional heavy snow totals were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals between 1 to 4 inches fell across Mower County with this winter system. The highest storm total snow accumulation in the county of 3.5 was measured 1 mile east-southeast of Austin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent north to northwest flow supported lake effect snow bands along the southern and southeastern shores of Lake of the Woods. Snowfall totals ranged from 5 inches to nearly 9 inches across the area. WSW004.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Member of the public estimated 8.75 inches of snow in Warroad MN. Storm Total Snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm Total Snow from the Lake of The Woods Sheriff's Office. Estimated between 6 and 7 inches fell during the evening of 12/8/2024. Report received the following morning, 12/9/2024.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills fell into the 40s below zero on the 11th and 12th of December in response to a strong Arctic high settling in over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gatzke MN DOT sensor measured wind chill of -43F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered clusters of storms resulted in large hail over portions of east-central Minnesota during the afternoon on July 13. One storm produced a brief tornado in Rice County. A line of storms then developed over northwestern Minnesota during the evening and travelled southeast across central Minnesota throughout the night. This line led to numerous reports of hail and wind damage before it weakened as it moved into Wisconsin.||An unusually warm and humid air mass resulted in heat index values greater than 100F across portions of southern and western Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of July 13. The combination of several thunderstorms and persistent cloud cover kept heat index values in the low to mid 80s further east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight trees down in the southeast bay of Watkins Lake. They were downed toward the west-northwest, which along with another report from the west side of Watkins Lake, is consistent with this having been due to a microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills fell into the 40s below zero on the 11th and 12th of December in response to a strong Arctic high settling in over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple AWOS and MN DOT sensors measured wind chills between -40F and -46F across Roseau Co the lowest of which was the MN DOT sensor near Badger at -46F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills fell into the 40s below zero on the 11th and 12th of December in response to a strong Arctic high settling in over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills measured at -42F and -43F in Williams and Roosevelt at MN DOT sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late evening hours of December 18th, a surface low pressure system began to move southeast out of the Dakotas. The system, originally characterized as a clipper due to its northwest to southeast motion, was unique in that it was ripe with Pacific moisture. Accumulating snow started during the early morning hours on December 19th, with most of the snow falling during the morning commute. A few deformation bands of light snow continued through the evening of December 19th.The end result was a widespread swath of 5-8��� of snow across south central Minnesota. ||Some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|8.9 inches of snowfall in Faribault, MN (Rice)|8.3 inches of snowfall 1 mile west of Elko New Market, MN (Scott)|8.0 inches of snowfall 5 miles north-northwest of Le Center (Le Sueur) |7.9 inches of snowfall in Henderson, MN (Sibley)|7.5 inches of snowfall in Excelsior, MN (Hennepin)|7.2 inches of snowfall in Oakdale, MN (Washington)|7.1 inches of snowfall in Welch, MN (Goodhue).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interpolated snowfall analysis from CoCoRaHS reports showed five to six inches of accumulation across the northeastern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system brought snowfall to much of northeastern Minnesota starting on the afternoon of the 3rd and persisting into the early morning hours of 5th. Snowfall totals were generally under 6 inches across the region, but some isolated higher amounts to 8 inches were reported in Cook County along the terrain ridge near Grand Marais.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated area of heavy snowfall occurred along the terrain ridge near Grand Marais during the passage of a clipper. While most accumulations in the region were under 6 inches, a high report of 8 inches was received from just north east of Grand Marais.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system brought snowfall to northeastern Minnesota starting the afternoon of the 8th and persisting into the morning of the 9th. Snowfall totals of 6 to 8 inches were reported with this system along with heavier amounts to around a foot along the higher terrain of along the North Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 5 to 7 inches were reported across Koochiching County with a high report of 7.8 inches at the official NWS observation location in International Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low brought freezing drizzle, rain, fog, sleet, and snow across central and southern Minnesota. Precipitation amounts were low, but the impacts were still notable with widespread traffic and property damages.||The Minnesota State Patrol stated that on Saturday afternoon on December 14th, there were seven injury crashes, 109 property damage crashes, 23 vehicles off the road, two spinouts, and a jackknifed semi.  One indirect fatality occurred in Inver Grove Heights (Dakota County) associated with the icy road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of freezing drizzle and freezing rain led to a thin layer of ice or glaze |over much of the Twin Cities metro area. There were numerous car accidents due to the icy conditions. One fatality occurred (indirectly) from icy road conditions in Inver Grove Heights, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 20th, off and on snow showers resulted in snow accumulations of less than one inch across portions of southeast Minnesota. As temperatures cooled during the evening hours, roads became snow covered and slippery. A vehicle accident led to 1 fatality and 3 injuries near mile marker 217 on Highway 14 in Olmsted County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of under 1 inch led to slippery road conditions. A head on vehicle collision near mile marker 217 on Highway 14 in Olmsted County resulted in one fatality and two non-life-threatening injuries in one vehicle, while the driver of the other vehicle also suffered life-threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours on December 14th, a vehicle rolled over on highway 57 resulting in non-life threatening injuries to the passengers. Minnesota State Patrol observed that the road was snow and ice covered at the time of the incident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle slid and eventually rolled off the road on Highway 57 in Dodge County resulting in two non-life threatening injuries. Minnesota State Patrol observed snow and ice covered roads at the time of the incident. A mix of snow and freezing rain had been reported at the ASOS and AWOS in Rochester and Dodge Center earlier that morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered clusters of storms resulted in large hail over portions of east-central Minnesota during the afternoon on July 13. One storm produced a brief tornado in Rice County. A line of storms then developed over northwestern Minnesota during the evening and travelled southeast across central Minnesota throughout the night. This line led to numerous reports of hail and wind damage before it weakened as it moved into Wisconsin.||An unusually warm and humid air mass resulted in heat index values greater than 100F across portions of southern and western Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of July 13. The combination of several thunderstorms and persistent cloud cover kept heat index values in the low to mid 80s further east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree snapped near the St. Paul Cathedral.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning line of showers and thunderstorms intensified during the afternoon upon encountering a favorable environment for severe thunderstorms. Steep mid-level lapse rates combined with strong instability. The storms produced damaging wind gusts which downed trees in several areas and mostly quarter to hen egg size hail, but some hail was much larger.||A hailstone in Stevens County measured six inches in diameter (it was photographed next to a ruler). This at least tied the record for the largest hailstone in Minnesota. Storm Data records also indicate six inch diameter hail in Nobles County on July 28, 1986, and in Murray County on July 4, 1968. The hail was collected about 13 to 15 minutes after the hail ended but was not saved in a freezer.

Areas of southwest Minnesota were on the southern edge of a band of snowfall which brushed areas north of Interstate 90, and was heaviest from 3 to 6 inches north of Highway 30. A majority of poor visibility conditions as low as one mile or less was caused by the snowfall which was largely ahead of the stronger winds with this system. Winds were strongest toward the South Dakota and Iowa borders with gusts as high as 35 to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple periods of snowfall totaled 3.5 inches 8 miles southeast of Hendricks.  While most of the snowfall occurred prior to onset of strongest winds, |some visibility of a half mile or less occurred during heavier snowfall.  Winds gusted at times from 25 to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of light snow moved across the region on November 20, 2024. The first area of snow was during the morning. Temperatures were dropping, and northwest winds were blowing 30 to 35 mph. The wet roads became ice covered, and over 500 traffic accidents were reported across the state of Minnesota. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across northeastern Minnesota during the month of November. The month began with D2 Severe Drought across most of the region. Improvement started early with portions of Pine and Carlton counties with the update on the 5th as D2 Severe Drought began to recede. Precipitation during the beginning and middle of the month led to widespread improvement with the update on the 19th which saw further reduction of D2 Severe Drought across portions of Lake, Cook, St. Louis, Itasca, Cass, Crow Wing, Aitkin, Carlton and Pine counties. The reduction trend continued with the final update for the month on the 26th with D2 Severe Drought becoming confined to portions of St. Louis, Cass and Itasca counties. Precipitation for the month was above normal across the region with the greatest departures from normal along the North Shore where upwards of 3 to 5 inches of above normal precipitation fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: November began with D2 Severe Drought conditions in place across all of northern Itasca County. Precipitation during the middle of the month lead to a slight improvement in the northeastern corner of the county where it was downgraded to D1 Moderate Drought with the update on the 26th. Overall precipitation for the month was around 0.5 to 1.5 inches above normal across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of light snow moved across the region on November 20, 2024. The first area of snow was during the morning. Temperatures were near or above freezing, and there were few impacts from this first round of snow. A second area of snow moved through during the late afternoon and early evening. Temperatures were dropping, and northwest winds were blowing 30 to 35 mph. The wet roads became ice covered, and over 500 traffic accidents were reported across the state of Minnesota. Many of these occurred in the Twin Cities metro area and surrounding locations. Although snowfall amounts were light (generally 1 to 2 inches), there were notable impacts to travel. ||Here are a few snowfall reports. |2.0 inches of snow fell near Richmond in Stearns County.|2.0 inches of snow fell near Andover in Anoka County.|1.9 inches of snow fell near Waseca in Waseca County.|1.5 inches of snow fell near New Prague in Le Sueur County.|1.1 inches of snow fell near Wayzata in Hennepin County.|1.0 inch of snow fell near Delano in Wright County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of 1 to 2.5 inches fell across portions of the county, mainly south and east. 2.3 inches of snow was measured 8.2 miles WSW of Saint Cloud, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across northeastern Minnesota during the month of November. The month began with D2 Severe Drought across most of the region. Improvement started early with portions of Pine and Carlton counties with the update on the 5th as D2 Severe Drought began to recede. Precipitation during the beginning and middle of the month led to widespread improvement with the update on the 19th which saw further reduction of D2 Severe Drought across portions of Lake, Cook, St. Louis, Itasca, Cass, Crow Wing, Aitkin, Carlton and Pine counties. The reduction trend continued with the final update for the month on the 26th with D2 Severe Drought becoming confined to portions of St. Louis, Cass and Itasca counties. Precipitation for the month was above normal across the region with the greatest departures from normal along the North Shore where upwards of 3 to 5 inches of above normal precipitation fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: November began with D2 Severe Drought conditions across most of northern St. Louis County with the exception of the northwest corner which was in D1 Moderate Drought. Precipitation during the beginning and middle of the month led to the complete removal of D2 Severe Drought conditions across the northern part of the county, leaving D1 Moderate Drought and a small area of D0 Abnormally Dry in its place. Overall precipitation for the month was around 0.5 to 1.5 inches above normal across the western part of the northern part of the county with up to 0.5 inches below normal across the eastern part.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting in excess of 50 mph during the early morning hours of the 11th led to widespread tree and power line damage across southern Cook County. Some of the downed power lines were reported to be sparking and smoking. During the height of the wind storm, the local power cooperative reported that 62 percent of its customers in the area were without power. Local public utility and highway departments spent the morning removing numerous trees across the area that had fallen onto roadways. The ASOS station at the Bay of Grand Marais reported a maximum wind gust of 55 mph just before 8AM during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting in excess of 50 mph during the early morning hours of the 11th led to widespread tree and power line damage across southern Cook County. Some of the downed power lines were reported to be sparking and smoking. During the height of the wind storm, the local power cooperative reported that 62 percent of its customers in the area were without power. The ASOS station at the Bay of Grand Marais reported a maximum wind gust of 55 mph just before 8AM during the event.

Catastrophic flooding occurred across parts of Minnesota in mid to late June 2024 as a stationary boundary stalled out across the Upper Midwest.||On June 19-21, several rounds of thunderstorms developed and tracked along this boundary, producing widespread 4 to 6 inches of rain across southern Minnesota, with some locations picking up 8 to 10 inches of rain near Mankato Minnesota.||There were dozens, if not hundreds of washouts along the roads in the counties that received heavy rain. In Watonwan County, the lift station at Madelia was lost due to flood, and nearly 15% of the homes were affected by flooding, with major damage to two homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 4 to 8 inches of rain fell from multiple rounds of thunderstorms. CoCoRaHS observers measured 8.10 inches near Mankato.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with low pressure proceeded through MN on June 12th. Early morning warm advection helped to spawn storms in the morning and midday hours. Instability increased across western MN during the afternoon to values between 2000 to 3000 J/kg, with shear between 40-50 kts. Scattered thunderstorms began to develop across western MN and move east. Across central MN, storms mainly produced damaging wind gusts, large hail, and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail measurement was provided by a broadcast meteorologist. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A noteworthy elevated layer of instability built up aloft on the afternoon of June 4th. A line of thunderstorms developed across west-central MN which moved into eastern MN. Low level shear profiles supported an environment capable of producing mesovortices along the line, with damaging wind gusts. A couple of funnel clouds were also spotted, but neither was confirmed to have made ground contact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were downed as well as some large branches broken off of trees. Two of the trees were blocking a road. There was also a public report of a tree that fell through a house. Another report from the same area via social media, showing photos of the downed trees. Time was estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with low pressure proceeded through MN on June 12th. Early morning warm advection helped to spawn storms in the morning and midday hours. Instability increased across western MN during the afternoon to values between 2000 to 3000 J/kg, with shear between 40-50 kts. Scattered thunderstorms began to develop across western MN and move east. Across central MN, storms mainly produced damaging wind gusts, large hail, and heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms moved across north-central and northeast Minnesota on the evening of Wednesday, June 12, 2024, producing large hail to the size of baseballs, isolated thunderstorm wind damage, and several tornadoes. Two distinct supercellular storms produced five tornadoes (rated EF-0 and EF-2) across portions of Crow Wing, Aitkin, and Carlton counties between roughly 6:15 PM and 7:30 PM. The supercell that tracked through Crow Wing and Aitkin County produced four separate tornadoes, first on/near Upper Whitefish Lake across to Clamshell Lake, or west of Crosslake, where a wide, sharp path of damage was found. This tornado dissipated, with a second tornado forming south of Wolford, west of Rabbit Lake (north of Crosby). This second tornado appeared to have a mainly continuous path of damage from near Wolford to Rabbit Lake to Cedar Lake to Prescott Lake. There may have been a break in this tornado as it traveled across the Crow Wing State Forest, but a combination of storm chaser and spotter video as well as ground surveys seemed to indicate a mainly consistent path of damage. A third tornado near the northern portion of Hammal Lake and continued east-southeast through Hanging Kettle Lake and Ripple Lake, where it destroyed a three-season cabin and boat house, toppled an 85 foot tall antenna tower, and uprooted and snapped numerous trees. This supercell then later produced a weak, wispy tornado with no known damage near Glen, MN, with the particular length of this tornado unknown due to limited access in this area. While this supercell was |ongoing, a separate supercell briefly produced a weak tornado in northwest Carlton County where mainly minor tree damage (large branches, a few trees snapped) was observed north of Wright, MN. No injuries or fatalities were reported, and NWS Duluth had a Tornado Warning in effect for these storms for the duration of time they produced tornadoes.||Another earlier storm that was the earliest supercell of the severe storm event, initially developed over Koochiching County and into far northern Saint Louis County where it split. The right split headed for Ely, MN, and continued on a southeast path towards Tofte. The supercell was rotating throughout its lifetime, with a report of a funnel cloud when the storm was east of Ely. The storm briefly weakened for a time, then strengthened and produced strong mid-level rotation northeast of Isabella. This storm then produced an EF-1 tornado starting near Tanner Lake to the northeast of Isabella that tracked southeast for just over four miles, creating a swath of numerous uprooted and snapped trees. This tornado rating was determined based on satellite imagery, and NWS Duluth is coordinating to learn more about any air or ground surveyed damage from our partners at the Superior National Forest which may result in a future revision in the tornado rating.||Another isolated storm developed during the late evening hours across north-central Minnesota and move into Itasca County after midnight. This storm produced ping-pong ball sized hail and downed some trees near Wirt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado damage initially appeared on the northeast shore of Tanner Lake (east of Dumbbell Lake), crossing Wanless Road (Forest Road 172) on a southeastward track. Satellite imagery depicts sporadic tree damage along the path, with a particularly impressive swath of trees that were uprooted or snapped east of East General Grade Road (Forest Road 362) measuring approximately 150 yards in width and a quarter mile in length. Damage continues to be evident for another mile or so before no additional discernible damage is evident. No fatalities or injuries were reported. Tornado rating was determined based on satellite imagery, and NWS Duluth is coordinating to learn more about any air or ground surveyed damage from our partners at the Superior National Forest which may result in a future revision in the tornado rating.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado developed on or near the Aitkin/Carlton County line and traveled southeast towards Highway 210 between Wright and Cromwell. Minor tree damage was noted on West Road near the start of the tornado path, with a broad area of large branches down and a few trees snapped towards the top. The tornado then traveled across a mainly marsh/bog landscape, crossing Center Road where power lines were damaged and widespread tree damage was noted along a 50 yard stretch of road. The tornado track continued on a southeast to east-southeast path, with the last signs of damage near to just south of Highway 210 near Woodbury Lake where a few clusters of tree damage were noted. No injuries or fatalities were reported, and property damage was very limited, with only a handful of homes/buildings impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday, June 18, 2024, a stationary surface front, in conjunction with an anomalously moist airmass over the Northland, resulting in widespread flash flooding. NWS Duluth issued seven Flash Flood Warnings (across much of NE MN and the Bayfield Peninsula), three of which were upgraded to include a considerable tag for life threatening flash flooding. Additionally, severe thunderstorms occurred ahead of the heaviest rainfall, prompting eight Tornado Warnings (near Ely and between Leech Lake and Grand Rapids) and 16 Severe Thunderstorm Warnings. Flash flooding extended from north-central Minnesota to the MN Arrowhead and across Lake Superior to the Bayfield Peninsula. River flooding would continue for weeks after the event, and high water in the Rainy River Basin headwaters would eventually lead to minor flooding after another round of heavy rain in early July. Furthermore, significant tree blowdowns occurred in the Superior National Forest due to damaging wind gusts associated with the storms.||Slow moving heavy rain and thunderstorms tracked across northeastern Minnesota and far northern Wisconsin in the afternoon and evening of Tuesday, June 18, leading to extensive damaging flash flooding that evening, and prolonged areal and river flooding through the end of the week. Scattered rain had been persistent through the day, and then widespread strong storms initiated along a slow moving front with parallel atmospheric flow in the upper atmosphere, allowing for persistent regeneration of thunderstorms over the same area. These storms formed within an environment that was tropical in nature, connected to a surge of moisture that originated in the Gulf of Mexico. This produced torrential downpours that were capable of producing rain rates of 1-5 inches per hour. Several reports from trusted CoCoRaHS observers across Northeast Minnesota reported accumulations of an inch in 15 to 30 minutes. A widespread area of 3 inches or more accumulated by the morning of Wednesday, June 19 - the majority of which had fallen in eight hours or less. Fifteen COOP or CoCoRaHS observers in Northeast Minnesota reported 5 inches or more, with the highest reports over 7 inches. MRMS estimates suggest that a small area of 8 inches or more may have been possible, in an area not verified with official reports. These reports of 5-7.5+ inches were 500 to 1000+ year rainfalls, with only a 0.1-0.2% chance of happening in any given year. Elsewhere, rainfall over 3 inches in a 6-12 hour period were 100-200 year rainfalls (0.5-1% chance in any given year). Full list of rainfall reports here, or below. The area had been primed for a big flash flooding event, thanks to a wet spring (top 10 wettest March-April-May on record in the Northland), and several moderate to heavy rain events earlier in the month of June.||The impacts of this heavy rain were immediate. During flash flooding on the evening of the 18th, roads across the Iron Range, Lake Vermilion area, Twin Ports, MN North Shore, and WI South Shore were quickly covered with standing and running water, closing and washing out Superior National Forest roads (including access roads to the Boundary Waters Canoe Wilderness, which would strand campers), County Highways (primarily across Cass, Itasca, St. Louis, Lake, Cook, and Bayfield Counties), state highways (notably MN Highways 61, 73, and 1 and WI Highway 13), and U.S. highways (including Highway 53 and 169 and Interstate 35). Homes along the Iron Range and near Lake Vermilion were surrounded by water and some washouts led to homes and businesses being cut off entirely. Numerous cars became stranded after driving into floodwaters. Debris washed onto roads, which made for treacherous nighttime driving conditions in combination with floodwaters. Culvert washouts led to crumbling roads under unsuspecting drivers. Severe thunderstorms occurring at the same time led to pockets of wind damage, one of which caused a tree to fall on a tent, injuring a nine year old boy and triggering a search and rescue operation in Cook County. The I-35 tunnels in Duluth filled with several feet of water through the evening and traffic was rerouted off the interstate. ||The next day, widespread overland flooding began to route into creeks, rivers, and lakes on top of the water that had already immediately fallen into area waterways, continuing extreme rises. Some rivers across the headwaters of the Rainy, Littlefork, Cloquet, and St. Louis rivers rose anywhere from 4 to 20 feet, causing rivers to quickly jump out of banks and lead to another period of minor to major flooding. Area inland lakes saw rises of 1 to 7 feet, especially along the Embarrass River chain of lakes near Biwabik, MN, where drinking water was partially affected. Major flooding occurred along the Littlefork River in the city of Cook, where floodwaters inundated downtown, surrounding residences and businesses with several feet of water for several days. Continued erosion from out-of-bank rivers and creeks continued to work away at area roads and highways, prompting additional road closures. High inflow from the Cloquet River led to water level rises on Island Lake Reservoir and subsequent rapid increase in outflow from the Island Lake Dam (up to 7387 cfs, greater than outflow released during historic flooding of June 2012). This led to extensive flooding downstream of the dam on the Cloquet River which inundated area roads and cut off driveways, encroached on railroad tracks, and surrounded private residences on Hunter and Bowman Lakes. Additional rises on the Mississippi River near Aitkin would briefly hit minor flood stage. Slow moving high water in the Rainy River basin eventually led to minor flooding on Crane, Namakan, and Kabetogama Lakes, thanks to an extra burst of heavy rain early in July.||Damage estimates from the event included $10.5 million in St. Louis County, $1.1 million in Itasca and Lake counties, $400 thousand in Aitkin County, and $205 thousand in Cass County. State and Federal disaster declarations were made for the hardest hit areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads in the area were entirely covered with at least 6 inches of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms moved across north-central and northeast Minnesota on the evening of Wednesday, June 12, 2024, producing large hail to the size of baseballs, isolated thunderstorm wind damage, and several tornadoes. Two distinct supercellular storms produced five tornadoes (rated EF-0 and EF-2) across portions of Crow Wing, Aitkin, and Carlton counties between roughly 6:15 PM and 7:30 PM. The supercell that tracked through Crow Wing and Aitkin County produced four separate tornadoes, first on/near Upper Whitefish Lake across to Clamshell Lake, or west of Crosslake, where a wide, sharp path of damage was found. This tornado dissipated, with a second tornado forming south of Wolford, west of Rabbit Lake (north of Crosby). This second tornado appeared to have a mainly continuous path of damage from near Wolford to Rabbit Lake to Cedar Lake to Prescott Lake. There may have been a break in this tornado as it traveled across the Crow Wing State Forest, but a combination of storm chaser and spotter video as well as ground surveys seemed to indicate a mainly consistent path of damage. A third tornado near the northern portion of Hammal Lake and continued east-southeast through Hanging Kettle Lake and Ripple Lake, where it destroyed a three-season cabin and boat house, toppled an 85 foot tall antenna tower, and uprooted and snapped numerous trees. This supercell then later produced a weak, wispy tornado with no known damage near Glen, MN, with the particular length of this tornado unknown due to limited access in this area. While this supercell was |ongoing, a separate supercell briefly produced a weak tornado in northwest Carlton County where mainly minor tree damage (large branches, a few trees snapped) was observed north of Wright, MN. No injuries or fatalities were reported, and NWS Duluth had a Tornado Warning in effect for these storms for the duration of time they produced tornadoes.||Another earlier storm that was the earliest supercell of the severe storm event, initially developed over Koochiching County and into far northern Saint Louis County where it split. The right split headed for Ely, MN, and continued on a southeast path towards Tofte. The supercell was rotating throughout its lifetime, with a report of a funnel cloud when the storm was east of Ely. The storm briefly weakened for a time, then strengthened and produced strong mid-level rotation northeast of Isabella. This storm then produced an EF-1 tornado starting near Tanner Lake to the northeast of Isabella that tracked southeast for just over four miles, creating a swath of numerous uprooted and snapped trees. This tornado rating was determined based on satellite imagery, and NWS Duluth is coordinating to learn more about any air or ground surveyed damage from our partners at the Superior National Forest which may result in a future revision in the tornado rating.||Another isolated storm developed during the late evening hours across north-central Minnesota and move into Itasca County after midnight. This storm produced ping-pong ball sized hail and downed some trees near Wirt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado initially formed near Wolford in Crow Wing County and entered Aitkin County near the end of Cedar Lake Drive on Cedar Lake. The tornado path was to the southeast, crossing 456th Place, 320th Street, and, finally, 310th Street before lifting. This last section of the tornado path after Cedar Lake shows damage to numerous trees via satellite imagery before the visible damage path ends just to the southwest of the intersection of 310th Street and Pioneer Avenue. The tornado was rates as an EF-0 in Aitkin County, but produced EF-2 damage in Crow Wing County just before crossing into Aitkin County. No fatalities or injuries were reported.
